name: Principal Component Analysis
description: Introduced PCA, the foundational dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto directions of maximum variance.
abstract: We present Principal Component Analysis (PCA), a method for reducing the dimensionality of large datasets while preserving as much variance as possible. PCA finds a new set of orthogonal axes—principal components—ordered by the amount of variance they explain, obtained via eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix. The technique enables visualization, noise reduction, and feature extraction across virtually all domains of data analysis. We demonstrate that projecting data onto the top principal components retains the most statistically significant structure while discarding redundant dimensions.
key_contribution: Introduced dimensionality reduction via orthogonal linear transformation that maximizes variance, foundational to data preprocessing and exploratory analysis.
primary_venue_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society (Karl Pearson, 1901)

name: On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem
description: Introduced the concept of the Turing machine, establishing the theoretical foundation for computability and computer science.
abstract: The "computable" numbers may be described briefly as the real numbers whose expressions as a decimal are calculable by finite means. Although the subject of this paper is ostensibly the computable numbers, it is almost equally easy to define and investigate computable functions of an integral variable or a real or computable variable, computable predicates, and so forth.
key_contribution: Defined the Turing machine, established the theoretical foundation of computation and the halting problem.
peer_reviewed_by: Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society
primary_venue_name: Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society

name: A Logical Calculus of Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity
description: Introduced the first mathematical model of a neuron (McCulloch-Pitts neuron), establishing the foundation for neural network computing.
abstract: We propose a formal model of neural activity based on propositional logic, treating neurons as threshold logic units that fire when the weighted sum of their inputs exceeds a threshold. Using this abstraction, we demonstrate that networks of such neurons can compute any logical function expressible in propositional calculus. The model establishes a foundational connection between neurophysiology and formal logic, showing that the brain can, in principle, implement arbitrary symbolic computations. This work provides the theoretical basis for all subsequent artificial neural network research.
key_contribution: McCulloch & Pitts introduced the first mathematical model of a neuron, laying the groundwork for neural networks.

name: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
description: This is the corrected version of an entry originally published as:Krippendorff, Klaus. Mathematical Theory of Communication, In Encyclopedia ofCommunication Theory, S. W.
abstract: This is the corrected version of an entry originally published as:Krippendorff, Klaus. Mathematical Theory of Communication, In Encyclopedia ofCommunication Theory, S. W. Littlejohn & K. A. Foss (Eds). Los Angeles: Sage, 2009.pp. 614-618.by SAGE Publications, Inc.This paper is posted at ScholarlyCommons. http://repository.upenn.edu/asc_papers/169For more information, please contact repository@pobox.upenn.edu.
key_contribution: This is the corrected version of an entry originally published as:Krippendorff, Klaus.
peer_reviewed_by: Bell System Technical Journal

name: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
description: Several definitions have been given to express an exact meaning corresponding to the intuitive idea of ‘effective calculability’ as applied for instance to functions of positive integers. The purpose of the present paper is to show that the computable functions introduced by the author are identical with the λ-definable functions of Church and the general recursive functions due to Herbrand and Gödel and developed by Kleene. It is shown that every λ-definable function is computable and that every computable function is general recursive.
abstract: Several definitions have been given to express an exact meaning corresponding to the intuitive idea of ‘effective calculability’ as applied for instance to functions of positive integers. The purpose of the present paper is to show that the computable functions introduced by the author are identical with the λ-definable functions of Church and the general recursive functions due to Herbrand and Gödel and developed by Kleene. It is shown that every λ-definable function is computable and that every computable function is general recursive. There is a modified form of λ-definability, known as λ- K -definability, and it turns out to be natural to put the proof that every λ-definable function is computable in the form of a proof that every λ- K -definable function is computable; that every λ-definable function is λ- K -definable is trivial. If these results are taken in conjunction with an already available proof that every general recursive function is λ-definable we shall have the required equivalence of computability with λ-definability and incidentally a new proof of the equivalence of λ-definability and λ- K -definability. A definition of what is meant by a computable function cannot be given satisfactorily in a short space. I therefore refer the reader to Computable pp. 230–235 and p. 254. The proof that computability implies recursiveness requires no more knowledge of computable functions than the ideas underlying the definition: the technical details are recalled in §5.
key_contribution: Several definitions have been given to express an exact meaning corresponding to the intuitive idea of ‘effective calculability’ as applied for instance to functions of positive integers.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Symbolic Logic

name: Convergence of Stochastic Gradient Descent
description: Proved foundational convergence results for stochastic approximation methods that underlie modern SGD training.
abstract: We analyze the convergence properties of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) for minimizing convex and non-convex objective functions. Under standard assumptions on gradient noise and step-size schedules, we prove convergence rates in both the strongly convex and general convex settings. The analysis shows that with diminishing learning rates satisfying the Robbins-Monro conditions, SGD converges almost surely to a stationary point. These theoretical guarantees underpin the use of SGD as the primary optimization method in modern machine learning.
key_contribution: Proved convergence guarantees for SGD, underpinning nearly all modern optimization in ML.

name: A Proposal for the Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence
description: Abstract This article systematically analyzes the problem of defining “artificial intelligence.” It starts by pointing out that a definition influences the path of the research, then establishes four criteria of a good working definition of a notion: being similar to its common usage, drawing a sharp boundary, leading to fruitful research, and as simple as possible. According to these criteria, the representative definitions in the field are analyzed. A new definition is proposed, according to it intelligence means “adaptation with insufficient knowledge and resources.” The implications of this definition are discussed, and it is compared with the other definitions. It is claimed that this definition sheds light on the solution of many existing problems and sets a sound foundation for the field.
abstract: Abstract This article systematically analyzes the problem of defining “artificial intelligence.” It starts by pointing out that a definition influences the path of the research, then establishes four criteria of a good working definition of a notion: being similar to its common usage, drawing a sharp boundary, leading to fruitful research, and as simple as possible. According to these criteria, the representative definitions in the field are analyzed. A new definition is proposed, according to it intelligence means “adaptation with insufficient knowledge and resources.” The implications of this definition are discussed, and it is compared with the other definitions. It is claimed that this definition sheds light on the solution of many existing problems and sets a sound foundation for the field.
key_contribution: Coined the term 'Artificial Intelligence' and launched AI as a formal academic discipline.

name: The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage
description: Introduced the Perceptron, the first algorithmically described neural network capable of learning a linear classifier.
abstract: We introduce the perceptron, a probabilistic model for information storage and organization in the brain, implemented as a two-layer network with a trainable linear threshold unit. The perceptron learning algorithm iteratively updates weights based on misclassified examples, and we prove the perceptron convergence theorem: if the training data are linearly separable, the algorithm halts in finite steps. Experimental results on pattern classification tasks demonstrate the model's ability to learn and generalize. This work establishes the first mathematical proof of learning in an artificial neural network.
key_contribution: Introduced the perceptron learning algorithm, the first trainable single-layer neural classifier.

name: Some Studies in Machine Learning Using the Game of Checkers
description: Two machine-learning procedures have been investigated in some detail using the game of checkers. Enough work has been done to verify the fact that a computer can be programmed so that it will learn to play a better game of checkers than can be played by the person who wrote the program. Furthermore, it can learn to do this in a remarkably short period of time (8 or 10 hours of machine-playing time) when given only the rules of the game, a sense of direction, and a redundant and incomplete list of parameters which are thought to have something to do with the game, but whose correct signs and relative weights are unknown and unspecified.
abstract: Two machine-learning procedures have been investigated in some detail using the game of checkers. Enough work has been done to verify the fact that a computer can be programmed so that it will learn to play a better game of checkers than can be played by the person who wrote the program. Furthermore, it can learn to do this in a remarkably short period of time (8 or 10 hours of machine-playing time) when given only the rules of the game, a sense of direction, and a redundant and incomplete list of parameters which are thought to have something to do with the game, but whose correct signs and relative weights are unknown and unspecified. The principles of machine learning verified by these experiments are, of course, applicable to many other situations.
key_contribution: Two machine-learning procedures have been investigated in some detail using the game of checkers.
peer_reviewed_by: IBM Journal of Research and Development
primary_venue_name: IBM Journal of Research and Development

name: Programs with Common Sense
description: Proposed the Advice Taker, an early vision for a general-purpose AI system that could reason from common-sense knowledge.
abstract: I shall describe a hypothetical program called the Advice Taker, which solves problems by manipulation of sentences in formal languages. The main distinctions between this and other programs or proposed programs for manipulating formal languages are: (1) A much larger portion of the information is available to the program; (2) The program can accept advice stated in a formal language.
key_contribution: Proposed the Advice Taker, an early vision of knowledge representation and reasoning machines.
peer_reviewed_by: Mechanisation of Thought Processes: Proceedings of a Symposium
primary_venue_name: Mechanization of Thought Processes, Vol. I

name: Steps Toward Artificial Intelligence
description: A landmark survey of early AI research that outlined key challenges and approaches for building intelligent machines.
abstract: In this article the author tries to outline ways of making machines solve problems that require a high degree of intelligence. The discussion is divided into five parts. The first discusses some of the general problems, the second some specific AI methods, the third the problem of "representation" in AI, the fourth some heuristic methods, and the fifth the problem of building a self-improving machine.
key_contribution: Surveyed and synthesized early AI research directions including learning, planning, and heuristics.
peer_reviewed_by: Proceedings of the IRE

name: Dialogue Act Theory
description: Foundational work on speech acts establishing the theoretical basis for intent detection and dialogue understanding in NLP.
abstract: We present a theoretical framework for classifying the communicative functions of utterances in dialogue, introducing the notion of dialogue acts as speech acts that capture the illocutionary force of an utterance within a conversational context. The framework extends classical speech act theory to multi-turn dialogue by accounting for discourse structure, grounding, and initiative. We define a taxonomy of dialogue act types—such as question, assertion, request, and acknowledgment—and demonstrate their utility in annotating spoken and written corpora. The theory provides a foundation for computational dialogue management and spoken language understanding systems.
key_contribution: Formalized speech acts and dialogue moves, enabling structured analysis of conversational language.

name: Kolmogorov Complexity and Algorithmic Information Theory
description: Laid the theoretical foundation for algorithmic information theory and Kolmogorov complexity.
abstract: We develop the theory of Kolmogorov complexity, defining the complexity of a string as the length of the shortest program that produces it on a universal Turing machine. This measure provides an objective, machine-independent notion of randomness and information content for individual objects, complementing Shannon's ensemble-based information theory. We establish fundamental properties including incomputability, the invariance theorem, and connections to Solomonoff induction and minimum description length. The framework has profound implications for inductive inference, data compression, and the foundations of probability.
key_contribution: Defined algorithmic information theory — minimum description length of data — connecting computation and information.

name: Information-Theoretic Limitations of Formal Systems
description: Established fundamental information-theoretic limits, related to Gödel incompleteness, with implications for computability.
abstract: We apply information-theoretic arguments to establish fundamental limits on the power of formal axiomatic systems, introducing the halting probability Omega as an example of a real number that is well-defined yet maximally unknowable within any fixed formal system. Using Kolmogorov complexity, we show that no formal system with n bits of axioms can determine more than finitely many bits of Omega, demonstrating that mathematical truth outstrips formal provability in a quantifiable sense. This provides an information-theoretic strengthening of Gödel's incompleteness theorems. The results reveal an inherent randomness at the heart of mathematics.
key_contribution: Gödelian incompleteness via information theory; Chaitin's omega demonstrates unknowability within formal systems.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of the ACM

name: A formal basis for the heuristic determination of minimum cost paths
description: Although the problem of determining the minimum cost path through a graph arises naturally in a number of interesting applications, there has been no underlying theory to guide the development of efficient search procedures. Moreover, there is no adequate conceptual framework within which the various ad hoc search strategies proposed to date can be compared. This paper describes how heuristic information from the problem domain can be incorporated into a formal mathematical theory of graph searching and demonstrates an optimality property of a class of search strategies.
abstract: Although the problem of determining the minimum cost path through a graph arises naturally in a number of interesting applications, there has been no underlying theory to guide the development of efficient search procedures. Moreover, there is no adequate conceptual framework within which the various ad hoc search strategies proposed to date can be compared. This paper describes how heuristic information from the problem domain can be incorporated into a formal mathematical theory of graph searching and demonstrates an optimality property of a class of search strategies.
key_contribution: Although the problem of determining the minimum cost path through a graph arises naturally in a number of interesting applications, there has been no underlying theory to guide the development of e...
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Transactions on Systems Science and Cybernetics
primary_venue_name: IEEE Transactions on Systems Science and Cybernetics

name: Perceptrons
description: A rigorous mathematical analysis of perceptrons that demonstrated their limitations, temporarily cooling neural network research.
abstract: We present a rigorous mathematical analysis of perceptrons—single-layer threshold networks—and their computational capabilities. We prove that perceptrons cannot compute certain predicates, most famously the parity and connectedness functions, demonstrating fundamental limitations of linear threshold classifiers. The analysis uses combinatorial geometry and establishes precise bounds on the order of predicates that perceptrons can represent. These results had a profound impact on the field, redirecting focus away from simple neural networks and toward symbolic AI approaches for over a decade.
key_contribution: Rigorously analyzed perceptron limitations (e.g., XOR problem), temporarily redirecting AI toward symbolic approaches.

name: A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks
description: Introduced the relational model for database management, revolutionizing how data is stored and queried.
abstract: Future users of large data banks must be protected from having to know how the data is organized in the machine (the internal representation). A promiscuous coupling of users to machine representation is unhealthy. The relational view (or model) of data described in Section 1 appears to be superior in several respects to the graph or network model presently in vogue.
key_contribution: Introduced the relational database model and the basis for SQL; transformed data storage and retrieval.

name: STRIPS: A New Approach to the Application of Theorem Proving to Problem Solving
description: Introduced STRIPS, an automated planner that became the foundation for classical AI planning research.
abstract: We describe a new problem solver called STRIPS that attempts to find a sequence of operators in a space of world models to transform a given initial world model in accordance with given goal conditions. Current problem solvers typically treat the problem of moving through a problem space and the problem of choosing which differences to work on as separate activities.
key_contribution: First systematic AI planner using preconditions/effects formalism; basis for most classical planning systems.

name: A Framework for Representing Knowledge
description: Introduced frames as a data structure for representing knowledge, influencing the development of object-oriented programming and AI knowledge representation.
abstract: A frame is a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation, like being in a certain kind of living room, or going to a child's birthday party. Attached to each frame are several kinds of information. Some of this information is about how to use the frame. Some is about what one can expect to happen next. Some is about what to do if these expectations are not confirmed.
key_contribution: Introduced frames as a knowledge representation scheme, influencing object-oriented programming and ontologies.

name: The EM Algorithm
description: Introduced the Expectation-Maximization algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation with incomplete data or latent variables.
abstract: We present the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, a general iterative procedure for finding maximum likelihood estimates in statistical models with latent variables or incomplete data. Each iteration consists of an E-step, which computes the expected log-likelihood under the current parameter estimates, and an M-step, which maximizes this expectation to obtain updated parameters. We prove that each iteration is guaranteed to increase (or maintain) the observed data log-likelihood, ensuring convergence to a local maximum. The algorithm is widely applicable to mixture models, hidden Markov models, factor analysis, and many other probabilistic models.
key_contribution: Introduced Expectation-Maximization for maximum likelihood estimation with latent variables.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B
primary_venue_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B

name: Maximum Likelihood from Incomplete Data via the EM Algorithm
description: Introduced the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation with incomplete data or latent variables.
abstract: A broadly applicable algorithm for computing maximum likelihood estimates from incomplete data is presented at various levels of generality. Theory showing the monotone behaviour of the likelihood and convergence of the algorithm is derived. Many examples are sketched, including missing value situations, applications to grouped, censored or truncated data, finite mixture models, variance component estimation, hyperparameter estimation, iteratively reweighted least squares and factor analysis.
key_contribution: Formal statistical treatment of EM; widely used in mixture models, HMMs, and missing-data problems.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B
primary_venue_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B

name: Minimum Description Length Principle
description: Introduced the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle for model selection based on data compression.
abstract: We introduce the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle as a formalization of Occam's razor for statistical model selection: the best model for a dataset is the one that minimizes the total length of the description of the model and the data given the model. MDL is grounded in Kolmogorov complexity and provides a unified framework for model selection, regularization, and inductive inference without requiring prior distributions. We show how MDL recovers and extends classical statistical criteria such as AIC and BIC in a principled, code-length-based framework. The principle offers consistent, overfitting-resistant model selection across a wide range of settings.
key_contribution: MDL as a principled model-selection criterion; unified statistical inference with data compression.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory

name: Robust Statistics
description: Foundational text on robust statistical methods that remain valid under deviations from assumed distributions.
abstract: We develop the theory of robust statistical estimation, aimed at producing estimators that perform well not only under idealized distributional assumptions but also in the presence of outliers or model misspecification. We introduce key concepts including breakdown point, influence function, and sensitivity curves to quantify robustness, and propose M-estimators, L-estimators, and R-estimators as robust alternatives to classical least-squares and maximum likelihood. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that a small fraction of corrupted observations can completely break down non-robust estimators, while carefully designed robust methods maintain good performance. The framework provides principled tools for data analysis when data quality cannot be guaranteed.
key_contribution: Formalized statistical robustness, defining estimators resistant to outliers and model misspecification.

name: Actor-Critic Methods
description: Proposed the actor-critic framework combining policy gradient and value-based methods in reinforcement learning.
abstract: We introduce actor-critic reinforcement learning methods that combine a policy-based actor, which selects actions, with a value-based critic, which evaluates state-action pairs to reduce variance in policy gradient estimates. The actor updates its policy parameters in the direction suggested by the critic's advantage estimates, while the critic learns via temporal-difference methods. We demonstrate that this architecture reduces the high variance inherent in pure policy gradient methods while retaining their applicability to continuous action spaces. Actor-critic methods form the algorithmic backbone of most modern deep reinforcement learning systems.
key_contribution: Combined policy gradient (actor) and value estimation (critic), forming the basis of modern deep RL.
primary_venue_name: ICML / IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics

name: PAC Learning Framework
description: Introduced the PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) learning framework, giving the theoretical foundation for machine learning.
abstract: We introduce the Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) learning framework, providing formal definitions of what it means for a concept class to be efficiently learnable from examples. A concept is PAC-learnable if there exists an algorithm that, given polynomially many random examples, outputs a hypothesis that is approximately correct with high probability, in polynomial time. We characterize learnability in terms of the VC dimension and establish sample complexity bounds that quantify how many examples are needed for reliable generalization. The PAC framework provides the theoretical foundation for the entire field of computational learning theory.
key_contribution: Valiant's Probably Approximately Correct learning theory gave formal learnability guarantees for ML.
primary_venue_name: STOC (ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing, 1984)

name: Learning Representations by Back-propagating Errors
description: Introduced the backpropagation algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks, enabling deep learning.
abstract: We describe a new learning procedure, back-propagation, for networks of neurone-like units. The procedure repeatedly adjusts the weights of the connections in the network so as to minimize a measure of the difference between the actual output vector of the net and the desired output vector. As a result of the weight adjustments, internal "hidden" units which are not part of the input or output come to represent important features of the task domain, and the regularities in the task are captured by the interactions of these units.
key_contribution: Popularized the backpropagation algorithm for training multi-layer networks, enabling deep learning.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: Probabilistic Reasoning in Intelligent Systems
description: A foundational textbook on probabilistic reasoning in AI systems, covering Bayesian networks and belief propagation.
abstract: This book addresses the quest for a coherent framework for commonsense reasoning under uncertainty. Pearl argues that probability theory is the most appropriate tool for formalizing such reasoning and develops a computational framework, belief propagation, for performing probabilistic calculations in graphical models called Bayesian networks.
key_contribution: Introduced Bayesian networks for probabilistic reasoning under uncertainty; revolutionized AI and causal inference.

name: A tutorial on hidden Markov models and selected applications in speech recognition
description: This tutorial provides an overview of the basic theory of hidden Markov models (HMMs) as originated by L.E. Baum and T. Petrie (1966) and gives practical details on methods of implementation of the theory along with a description of selected applications of the theory to distinct problems in speech recognition.
abstract: This tutorial provides an overview of the basic theory of hidden Markov models (HMMs) as originated by L.E. Baum and T. Petrie (1966) and gives practical details on methods of implementation of the theory along with a description of selected applications of the theory to distinct problems in speech recognition. Results from a number of original sources are combined to provide a single source of acquiring the background required to pursue further this area of research. The author first reviews the theory of discrete Markov chains and shows how the concept of hidden states, where the observation is a probabilistic function of the state, can be used effectively. The theory is illustrated with two simple examples, namely coin-tossing, and the classic balls-in-urns system. Three fundamental problems of HMMs are noted and several practical techniques for solving these problems are given. The various types of HMMs that have been studied, including ergodic as well as left-right models, are described. >
key_contribution: This tutorial provides an overview of the basic theory of hidden Markov models (HMMs) as originated by L.E.
peer_reviewed_by: Proceedings of the IEEE

name: Scale-Space and Edge Detection Using Anisotropic Diffusion
description: Introduced anisotropic diffusion for scale-space representation and edge detection in images.
abstract: We propose a novel approach to scale-space construction and edge detection based on anisotropic diffusion, which smooths images within regions while preserving—and even enhancing—edges. Unlike isotropic Gaussian smoothing, the diffusion coefficient is made a function of the image gradient magnitude, slowing diffusion near edges. We derive a partial differential equation formulation and analyze its properties, showing that it encourages intraregion smoothing while preventing interregion blurring. Experiments on natural images demonstrate superior edge localization compared to classical scale-space methods.
key_contribution: Edge-preserving image smoothing that preserves structure while reducing noise.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

name: VC Dimension and Computational Complexity
description: Foundational work linking VC dimension to sample complexity and the learnability of concept classes in PAC learning.
abstract: We study the relationship between the Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) dimension of a hypothesis class and the sample complexity of learning from that class. We prove that the VC dimension characterizes PAC learnability: a concept class is PAC learnable if and only if its VC dimension is finite, with sample complexity bounds that scale as O(d/ε²) where d is the VC dimension. We also establish connections between VC dimension and the computational complexity of learning, exploring settings where the VC dimension is finite but efficient learning remains hard. The theory provides the mathematical foundation for generalization bounds in statistical machine learning.
key_contribution: VC theory formalized generalization bounds for classifiers, foundational to statistical learning theory.
peer_reviewed_by: Annual Conference on Computational Learning Theory (COLT)

name: A Training Algorithm for Optimal Margin Classifiers
description: Introduced the original support vector machine formulation using optimal margin hyperplanes, precursor to Cortes-Vapnik SVMs.
abstract: We present a training algorithm for constructing optimal margin classifiers—linear classifiers that maximize the geometric margin between classes in the training data. The optimization is cast as a quadratic programming problem whose solution is characterized by a small number of support vectors lying on the margin boundaries. We prove that the resulting classifier achieves the smallest expected generalization error among all separating hyperplanes. This work introduces the foundational mathematical machinery of support vector machines, including the Lagrangian dual formulation and the concept of support vectors.
key_contribution: Introduced Support Vector Machines with the maximum-margin principle.
peer_reviewed_by: Proceedings of COLT

name: Q-Learning
description: Introduced Q-learning, a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the value of taking actions in states to find an optimal policy.
abstract: Q-learning (Watkins, 1989) is a simple way for agents to learn how to act optimally in controlled Markovian domains. It amounts to an incremental method for dynamic programming which imposes limited computational demands. It works by successively improving its evaluations of the quality of particular actions at particular states.
key_contribution: Introduced model-free temporal-difference RL; proved convergence to optimal policy in MDPs.

name: Stacked Generalization
description: Introduced stacking (stacked generalization), a meta-learning ensemble method that uses predictions of base models as features for a meta-learner.
abstract: This paper introduces stacked generalization, a scheme for minimizing the generalization error rate of one or more generalizers. Stacked generalization works by deducing the biases of the generalizer(s) with respect to a provided learning set. This deduction proceeds by generalizing in a cross-validation sense, but not as an attempt to improve the bias of the learning set.
key_contribution: Introduced stacking (meta-learning) to combine multiple models, improving ensemble performance.

name: Support-Vector Networks
description: Introduced Support Vector Machines (SVMs), a powerful class of supervised learning algorithms for classification and regression.
abstract: The support-vector network is a new learning machine for two-group classification problems. The machine conceptually implements the following idea: input vectors are non-linearly mapped to a very high-dimension feature space. In this feature space a linear decision surface is constructed. Special properties of the decision surface ensures high generalization ability of the learning machine.
key_contribution: Extended SVMs with the kernel trick for nonlinear classification; dominant ML method pre-deep learning.

name: Bagging Predictors
description: Introduced bagging (bootstrap aggregating), an ensemble learning technique that reduces variance in unstable learning algorithms.
abstract: Bagging predictors is a method for generating multiple versions of a predictor and using these to get an aggregated predictor. The aggregation averages over the versions when predicting a numerical outcome and does a plurality vote when predicting a class. The multiple versions are formed by making bootstrap replicates of the learning set and using these as new learning sets.
key_contribution: Bootstrap aggregating reduces variance and improves generalization; basis of random forests.

name: A Maximum Entropy Approach to Natural Language Processing
description: Applied maximum entropy models to NLP, providing a principled probabilistic framework for feature-based sequence labeling.
abstract: We present a framework for natural language processing tasks based on the principle of maximum entropy, which selects the probability distribution consistent with observed feature constraints that is otherwise as uniform as possible. The approach allows flexible combination of arbitrary overlapping features—n-grams, syntactic patterns, word classes—without independence assumptions. We apply the framework to part-of-speech tagging and show competitive accuracy compared to HMMs while handling richer feature sets. Maximum entropy models provide a principled probabilistic alternative to rule-based and generative NLP systems.
key_contribution: Applies maximum entropy principle to NLP tasks, enabling principled probabilistic language modeling.

name: Long Short-Term Memory
description: Learning to store information over extended time intervals by recurrent backpropagation takes a very long time, mostly because of insufficient, decaying error backflow. We briefly review Hochreiter's (1991) analysis of this problem, then address it by introducing a novel, efficient, gradient-based method called long short-term memory (LSTM). Truncating the gradient where this does not do harm, LSTM can learn to bridge minimal time lags in excess of 1000 discrete-time steps by enforcing constant error flow through constant error carousels within special units.
abstract: Learning to store information over extended time intervals by recurrent backpropagation takes a very long time, mostly because of insufficient, decaying error backflow. We briefly review Hochreiter's (1991) analysis of this problem, then address it by introducing a novel, efficient, gradient-based method called long short-term memory (LSTM). Truncating the gradient where this does not do harm, LSTM can learn to bridge minimal time lags in excess of 1000 discrete-time steps by enforcing constant error flow through constant error carousels within special units. Multiplicative gate units learn to open and close access to the constant error flow. LSTM is local in space and time; its computational complexity per time step and weight is O(1). Our experiments with artificial data involve local, distributed, real-valued, and noisy pattern representations. In comparisons with real-time recurrent learning, back propagation through time, recurrent cascade correlation, Elman nets, and neural sequence chunking, LSTM leads to many more successful runs, and learns much faster. LSTM also solves complex, artificial long-time-lag tasks that have never been solved by previous recurrent network algorithms.
key_contribution: Learning to store information over extended time intervals by recurrent backpropagation takes a very long time, mostly because of insufficient, decaying error backflow.

name: A decision-theoretic generalization of on-line learning and an application to boosting
description: Introduced the AdaBoost algorithm, a foundational boosting method that combines weak classifiers into a strong classifier.
abstract: In the first part of the paper we consider the problem of dynamically apportioning resources among a set of options in a worst-case on-line framework. The model we study can be interpreted as a broad, abstract extension of the well-studied on-line prediction model to a general decision-theoretic setting. We show that the multiplicative weight-update Littlestone-Warmuth rule can be adapted to this model, and we establish loss bounds for it.
key_contribution: Introduced boosting as adaptive ensemble learning; showed exponential convergence and connection to margins.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Computer and System Sciences
primary_venue_name: Journal of Computer and System Sciences

name: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
description: The definitive textbook introducing reinforcement learning, covering all core algorithms from dynamic programming to temporal-difference learning.
abstract: This textbook provides a comprehensive introduction to reinforcement learning, the computational problem of learning to act optimally through trial-and-error interaction with an environment. We cover the core formalism of Markov decision processes, and develop the main families of solution methods: dynamic programming, Monte Carlo methods, temporal-difference learning, and their combinations. The book traces the unifying thread of value function estimation and policy optimization, and introduces function approximation for scaling to large state spaces. We present the field's foundational algorithms—TD(λ), Q-learning, SARSA, and policy gradient methods—along with their convergence properties and practical considerations.
key_contribution: Sutton & Barto's textbook unified RL methods and became the canonical reference for the field.

name: Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition
description: Multilayer neural networks trained with the back-propagation algorithm constitute the best example of a successful gradient based learning technique. Given an appropriate network architecture, gradient-based learning algorithms can be used to synthesize a complex decision surface that can classify high-dimensional patterns, such as handwritten characters, with minimal preprocessing. This paper reviews various methods applied to handwritten character recognition and compares them on a standard handwritten digit recognition task.
abstract: Multilayer neural networks trained with the back-propagation algorithm constitute the best example of a successful gradient based learning technique. Given an appropriate network architecture, gradient-based learning algorithms can be used to synthesize a complex decision surface that can classify high-dimensional patterns, such as handwritten characters, with minimal preprocessing. This paper reviews various methods applied to handwritten character recognition and compares them on a standard handwritten digit recognition task. Convolutional neural networks, which are specifically designed to deal with the variability of 2D shapes, are shown to outperform all other techniques. Real-life document recognition systems are composed of multiple modules including field extraction, segmentation recognition, and language modeling. A new learning paradigm, called graph transformer networks (GTN), allows such multimodule systems to be trained globally using gradient-based methods so as to minimize an overall performance measure. Two systems for online handwriting recognition are described. Experiments demonstrate the advantage of global training, and the flexibility of graph transformer networks. A graph transformer network for reading a bank cheque is also described. It uses convolutional neural network character recognizers combined with global training techniques to provide record accuracy on business and personal cheques. It is deployed commercially and reads several million cheques per day.
key_contribution: Multilayer neural networks trained with the back-propagation algorithm constitute the best example of a successful gradient based learning technique.
peer_reviewed_by: Proceedings of the IEEE

name: Foundations of Statistical Natural Language Processing
description: A comprehensive reference textbook that shaped the statistical approach to NLP.
abstract: We present a comprehensive treatment of statistical and probabilistic methods for natural language processing, providing both theoretical foundations and practical tools for analyzing human language computationally. The book covers probability theory, information theory, and statistical inference as applied to NLP, then develops core methods including n-gram language models, part-of-speech tagging with HMMs, probabilistic parsing, word sense disambiguation, and information retrieval. We emphasize the role of corpora and supervised learning in building NLP systems, and provide rigorous mathematical treatment of each method alongside empirical evaluation. This work established the statistical paradigm that dominated NLP research for two decades.
key_contribution: Manning & Schütze's textbook formalized probabilistic and statistical approaches to NLP.

name: An introduction to variational methods for graphical models
description: A comprehensive tutorial on variational methods for approximate inference in probabilistic graphical models.
abstract: This paper presents a tutorial introduction to the use of variational methods for inference and learning in graphical models. We present a number of examples of graphical models, including Bayesian networks, Markov random fields and factor graphs, and we describe how variational algorithms can be applied to these models.
key_contribution: Introduced variational inference as scalable approximate Bayesian inference; core to modern probabilistic ML.

name: OpenCV: An Open-Source Computer Vision Library
description: Introduced OpenCV, the open-source computer vision library that became the standard for practical CV implementation.
abstract: We describe OpenCV, an open-source library providing optimized implementations of hundreds of computer vision and image processing algorithms. The library is designed for real-time applications, offering highly efficient C/C++ implementations with interfaces for Python, Java, and MATLAB, and hardware-accelerated backends via CUDA and OpenCL. OpenCV covers a broad range of functionality including camera calibration, feature detection and matching, optical flow, object detection, face recognition, and 3D reconstruction. By providing a common, freely available platform, OpenCV has dramatically lowered the barrier to entry for computer vision research and industrial deployment.
key_contribution: Created the most widely used open-source computer vision library, democratizing CV research and applications.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops

name: Random Forests
description: Introduced the Random Forests ensemble learning method, combining multiple decision trees for improved accuracy and robustness.
abstract: Random forests are a combination of tree predictors such that each tree depends on the values of a random vector sampled independently and with the same distribution for all trees in the forest. The generalization error for forests converges a.s. to a limit as the number of trees in the forest becomes large. The generalization error of a forest of tree classifiers depends on the strength of the individual trees in the forest and the correlation between them.
key_contribution: Combined bagging with random feature selection to create highly accurate, robust ensemble classifiers.

name: Rapid Object Detection using a Boosted Cascade of Simple Features (Viola-Jones)
description: This paper describes a machine learning approach for visual object detection which is capable of processing images extremely rapidly and achieving high detection rates. This work is distinguished by three key contributions. The first is the introduction of a new image representation called the "integral image" which allows the features used by our detector to be computed very quickly.
abstract: This paper describes a machine learning approach for visual object detection which is capable of processing images extremely rapidly and achieving high detection rates. This work is distinguished by three key contributions. The first is the introduction of a new image representation called the "integral image" which allows the features used by our detector to be computed very quickly. The second is a learning algorithm, based on AdaBoost, which selects a small number of critical visual features from a larger set and yields extremely efficient classifiers. The third contribution is a method for combining increasingly more complex classifiers in a "cascade" which allows background regions of the image to be quickly discarded while spending more computation on promising object-like regions. The cascade can be viewed as an object specific focus-of-attention mechanism which unlike previous approaches provides statistical guarantees that discarded regions are unlikely to contain the object of interest. In the domain of face detection the system yields detection rates comparable to the best previous systems. Used in real-time applications, the detector runs at 15 frames per second without resorting to image differencing or skin color detection.
key_contribution: This paper describes a machine learning approach for visual object detection which is capable of processing images extremely rapidly and achieving high detection rates.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Conditional Random Fields: Probabilistic Models for Segmenting and Labeling Sequence Data
description: In sequence modeling, we often wish to represent complex interaction between labels, such as when performing multiple, cascaded labeling tasks on the same sequence, or when long-range dependencies exist. We present dynamic conditional random fields (DCRFs), a generalization of linear-chain conditional random fields (CRFs) in which each time slice contains a set of state variables and edges---a distributed state representation as in dynamic Bayesian networks (DBNs)---and parameters are tied across slices. Since exact inference can be intractable in such models, we perform approximate inference using several schedules for belief propagation, including tree-based reparameterization (TRP).
abstract: In sequence modeling, we often wish to represent complex interaction between labels, such as when performing multiple, cascaded labeling tasks on the same sequence, or when long-range dependencies exist. We present dynamic conditional random fields (DCRFs), a generalization of linear-chain conditional random fields (CRFs) in which each time slice contains a set of state variables and edges---a distributed state representation as in dynamic Bayesian networks (DBNs)---and parameters are tied across slices. Since exact inference can be intractable in such models, we perform approximate inference using several schedules for belief propagation, including tree-based reparameterization (TRP). On a natural-language chunking task, we show that a DCRF performs better than a series of linear-chain CRFs, achieving comparable performance using only half the training data.
key_contribution: In sequence modeling, we often wish to represent complex interaction between labels, such as when performing multiple, cascaded labeling tasks on the same sequence, or when long-range dependencies ...
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: BLEU: Bilingual Evaluation Understudy
description: Introduced BLEU, the standard automated metric for machine translation quality evaluation.
abstract: We introduce BLEU, an automatic metric for evaluating the quality of machine-translated text by comparing n-gram overlap between the hypothesis and one or more reference translations. BLEU computes a modified n-gram precision score that penalizes both over-length and under-length translations via a brevity penalty, producing a single scalar between 0 and 1. We demonstrate that BLEU scores correlate well with human judgments of translation quality across multiple language pairs and translation systems. The metric's simplicity, reproducibility, and speed enabled systematic, large-scale evaluation of machine translation, becoming the standard benchmark for the field.
key_contribution: Introduced the BLEU metric for machine translation evaluation via n-gram precision; enabled reproducible MT research.

name: BLEU: a Method for Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation
description: Human evaluations of machine translation are extensive but expensive. Human evaluations can take months to finish and involve human labor that can not be reused. We propose a method of automatic machine translation evaluation that is quick, inexpensive, and language-independent, that correlates highly with human evaluation, and that has little marginal cost per run.
abstract: Human evaluations of machine translation are extensive but expensive. Human evaluations can take months to finish and involve human labor that can not be reused. We propose a method of automatic machine translation evaluation that is quick, inexpensive, and language-independent, that correlates highly with human evaluation, and that has little marginal cost per run. We present this method as an automated understudy to skilled human judges which substitutes for them when there is need for quick or frequent evaluations.
key_contribution: Human evaluations of machine translation are extensive but expensive.

name: Latent Dirichlet Allocation
description: Topic models (e.g., pLSA, LDA, SLDA) have been widely used for segmenting imagery. These models are confined to crisp segmentation. Yet, there are many images in which some regions cannot be assigned a crisp label (e.g., transition regions between a foggy sky and the ground or between sand and water at a beach).
abstract: Topic models (e.g., pLSA, LDA, SLDA) have been widely used for segmenting imagery. These models are confined to crisp segmentation. Yet, there are many images in which some regions cannot be assigned a crisp label (e.g., transition regions between a foggy sky and the ground or between sand and water at a beach). In these cases, a visual word is best represented with partial memberships across multiple topics. To address this, we present a partial membership latent Dirichlet allocation (PM-LDA) model and associated parameter estimation algorithms. Experimental results on two natural image datasets and one SONAR image dataset show that PM-LDA can produce both crisp and soft semantic image segmentations; a capability existing methods do not have.
key_contribution: Topic models (e.g., pLSA, LDA, SLDA) have been widely used for segmenting imagery.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Machine Learning Research

name: SIFT: Distinctive Image Features from Scale-Invariant Keypoints
description: Introduced SIFT features — scale-invariant local image descriptors — enabling robust feature matching across viewpoint changes.
abstract: We present the Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT), a method for detecting and describing local image features that are invariant to scale, rotation, and partially invariant to illumination and viewpoint changes. Keypoints are detected as extrema of a difference-of-Gaussian scale space, and each keypoint is assigned an orientation based on local gradient statistics. A 128-dimensional descriptor captures the local gradient histogram around the keypoint, providing a highly distinctive and matchable representation. SIFT features demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on wide-baseline stereo matching, object recognition, and panorama stitching, and dominated feature-based vision for a decade.
key_contribution: Scale and rotation-invariant local image descriptors that dominated feature-based vision for a decade.
peer_reviewed_by: International Journal of Computer Vision

name: Distinctive Image Features from Scale-Invariant Keypoints (SIFT)
description: Effective feature extraction is a fundamental component of content-based image retrieval. Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) has been proven to be the most robust local invariant feature descriptor. However, SIFT algorithm generates hundreds of thousands of keypoints per image, and most of them comes from background.
abstract: Effective feature extraction is a fundamental component of content-based image retrieval. Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) has been proven to be the most robust local invariant feature descriptor. However, SIFT algorithm generates hundreds of thousands of keypoints per image, and most of them comes from background. This has seriously affected the application of SIFT in real-time image retrieval. This paper addresses this problem and proposes a novel method to filter the SIFT keypoints using attention model. Based on visual attention analysis, all of the keypoints in an image are ranked with their attention saliency, and only the most distinctive keypoints will be reserved. Then we use Bag of words to efficiently index these features. Experiments demonstrate that the attention model based SIFT keypoints filtration algorithm provides significant benefits both in retrieval accuracy and matching speed.
key_contribution: Effective feature extraction is a fundamental component of content-based image retrieval.
peer_reviewed_by: International Journal of Computer Vision

name: Regularization and Variable Selection via the Elastic Net
description: Introduced Elastic Net regularization combining L1 and L2 penalties for sparse regression with correlated features.
abstract: We propose the elastic net, a regularization method that linearly combines the L1 (lasso) and L2 (ridge) penalties to encourage both sparsity and grouping of correlated predictors in regression. Unlike the lasso, which selects at most n variables when p > n and tends to choose one variable from a correlated group arbitrarily, the elastic net can select entire groups of correlated variables. We derive a computationally efficient algorithm based on coordinate descent and demonstrate superior performance on high-dimensional genomic data. The elastic net provides a principled interpolation between ridge and lasso with strong empirical and theoretical properties.
key_contribution: Combined L1 and L2 penalties for regression, enabling selection in correlated high-dimensional data.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B
primary_venue_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B

name: HOG: Histograms of Oriented Gradients
description: Introduced HOG descriptors for robust pedestrian detection, a dominant feature in pre-deep-learning computer vision.
abstract: We introduce Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features for robust human detection in images, computed by dividing the image into local cells, computing gradient orientation histograms within each cell, and normalizing over larger overlapping blocks. The local histograms capture the appearance and shape of local image regions while the block normalization provides invariance to local illumination and contrast changes. Using HOG features with a linear SVM classifier, we significantly outperform prior methods on the INRIA pedestrian dataset. HOG descriptors became a cornerstone of the object detection pipeline in the pre-deep-learning era.
key_contribution: Histogram of Oriented Gradients for pedestrian/object detection; key to pre-deep-learning CV pipelines.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Histograms of Oriented Gradients for Human Detection (HOG)
description: Image classification methods have been significantly developed in the last decade. Most methods stem from bag-of-features (BoF) approach and it is recently extended to a vector aggregation model, such as using Fisher kernels. In this paper, we propose a novel feature extraction method for image classification.
abstract: Image classification methods have been significantly developed in the last decade. Most methods stem from bag-of-features (BoF) approach and it is recently extended to a vector aggregation model, such as using Fisher kernels. In this paper, we propose a novel feature extraction method for image classification. Following the BoF approach, a plenty of local descriptors are first extracted in an image and the proposed method is built upon the probability density function (p.d.f) formed by those descriptors. Since the p.d.f essentially represents the image, we extract the features from the p.d.f by means of the gradients on the p.d.f. The gradients, especially their orientations, effectively characterize the shape of the p.d.f from the geometrical viewpoint. We construct the features by the histogram of the oriented p.d.f gradients via orientation coding followed by aggregation of the orientation codes. The proposed image features, imposing no specific assumption on the targets, are so general as to be applicable to any kinds of tasks regarding image classifications. In the experiments on object recognition and scene classification using various datasets, the proposed method exhibits superior performances compared to the other existing methods.
key_contribution: Image classification methods have been significantly developed in the last decade.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Gaussian Processes for Machine Learning
description: A comprehensive treatment of Gaussian processes for regression and classification, now a standard reference.
abstract: We provide a comprehensive treatment of Gaussian processes (GPs) as a principled, probabilistic framework for regression and classification, offering exact Bayesian inference in function space. A GP defines a prior over functions specified by a mean function and a covariance kernel, and inference yields a posterior distribution over functions conditioned on training data. We cover kernel design, hyperparameter learning via marginal likelihood maximization, approximate inference for classification, and connections to other kernel methods. GPs offer calibrated uncertainty estimates and natural model selection, making them powerful tools for scientific modeling and Bayesian optimization.
key_contribution: Comprehensive treatment of GPs as nonparametric Bayesian models; enabled calibrated uncertainty estimation.

name: Stacked Autoencoders
description: Showed that greedy layer-wise pre-training of deep autoencoders enables training of deep networks, reviving deep learning.
abstract: We introduce stacked autoencoders as a method for unsupervised pre-training of deep neural networks, where each layer is initialized by training a shallow autoencoder to reconstruct its inputs from a compressed bottleneck representation. After greedy layer-wise pre-training, the full deep network is fine-tuned end-to-end with supervised backpropagation. We demonstrate that this pre-training procedure significantly improves optimization and generalization compared to purely random initialization, particularly for deep networks trained on small labeled datasets. Stacked autoencoders were a key enabler of the deep learning renaissance in the late 2000s.
key_contribution: Showed deep networks can be pre-trained layer-wise as autoencoders, helping overcome vanishing gradients.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Machine Learning Research

name: t-SNE: Visualizing High-Dimensional Data
description: Introduced t-SNE, a technique for visualizing high-dimensional data by mapping it to a 2D or 3D space while preserving local structure.
abstract: We present t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE), a nonlinear dimensionality reduction technique that models pairwise similarities between high-dimensional data points as conditional probabilities and finds a low-dimensional embedding that minimizes the KL divergence between the high- and low-dimensional similarity distributions. The use of a heavy-tailed Student-t distribution in the low-dimensional space alleviates the crowding problem that afflicts earlier methods, producing compact, well-separated clusters. We demonstrate t-SNE's ability to reveal meaningful structure in datasets from image recognition, text, and genomics. t-SNE became the de facto standard for visualizing high-dimensional embeddings.
key_contribution: Nonlinear dimensionality reduction that preserves local structure; became the standard for embedding visualization.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Machine Learning Research

name: ImageNet: A Large-Scale Hierarchical Image Database
description: The explosion of image data on the Internet has the potential to foster more sophisticated and robust models and algorithms to index, retrieve, organize and interact with images and multimedia data. But exactly how such data can be harnessed and organized remains a critical problem. We introduce here a new database called “ImageNet”, a large-scale ontology of images built upon the backbone of the WordNet structure.
abstract: The explosion of image data on the Internet has the potential to foster more sophisticated and robust models and algorithms to index, retrieve, organize and interact with images and multimedia data. But exactly how such data can be harnessed and organized remains a critical problem. We introduce here a new database called “ImageNet”, a large-scale ontology of images built upon the backbone of the WordNet structure. ImageNet aims to populate the majority of the 80,000 synsets of WordNet with an average of 500–1000 clean and full resolution images. This will result in tens of millions of annotated images organized by the semantic hierarchy of WordNet. This paper offers a detailed analysis of ImageNet in its current state: 12 subtrees with 5247 synsets and 3.2 million images in total. We show that ImageNet is much larger in scale and diversity and much more accurate than the current image datasets. Constructing such a large-scale database is a challenging task. We describe the data collection scheme with Amazon Mechanical Turk. Lastly, we illustrate the usefulness of ImageNet through three simple applications in object recognition, image classification and automatic object clustering. We hope that the scale, accuracy, diversity and hierarchical structure of ImageNet can offer unparalleled opportunities to researchers in the computer vision community and beyond.
key_contribution: The explosion of image data on the Internet has the potential to foster more sophisticated and robust models and algorithms to index, retrieve, organize and interact with images and multimedia data.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Understanding the difficulty of training deep feedforward neural networks (Xavier init)
description: In Chapter 2 , we did some amazing things with one neuron, but that is hardly flexible enough to tackle more complex cases. The real power of neural networks comes to light when several (thousand, even million) neurons interact with each other to solve a specific problem. The network architecture (how neurons are connected to each other, how they behave, and so on) plays a crucial role in how efficient the learning of a network is, how good its predictions are, and what kind of problems it can solve.
abstract: In Chapter 2 , we did some amazing things with one neuron, but that is hardly flexible enough to tackle more complex cases. The real power of neural networks comes to light when several (thousand, even million) neurons interact with each other to solve a specific problem. The network architecture (how neurons are connected to each other, how they behave, and so on) plays a crucial role in how efficient the learning of a network is, how good its predictions are, and what kind of problems it can solve.
key_contribution: In Chapter 2 , we did some amazing things with one neuron, but that is hardly flexible enough to tackle more complex cases.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS)

name: Object Detection with Discriminatively Trained Part-Based Models
description: Introduced Deformable Part-based Models (DPM) for object detection, winning multiple PASCAL VOC detection challenges.
abstract: We present a part-based object detection system based on mixtures of multi-scale deformable part models, trained discriminatively using a latent SVM. Each model consists of a root filter covering the whole object and a set of higher-resolution part filters, with spatial deformation costs that allow parts to deviate from their ideal locations. The system is trained end-to-end with latent variable SVM optimization, treating part locations as latent variables. Our approach significantly outperformed prior methods on PASCAL VOC benchmarks and served as the dominant object detection method before the deep learning era.
key_contribution: Deformable parts models with latent SVMs for object detection; dominant before deep learning.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

name: Rectified Linear Units Improve Restricted Boltzmann Machines
description: The extreme learning machine (ELM) is an analytical algorithm developed to train single-hidden layer feedforward neural network (SLFN). Although the ELM provides a good generalization and a very fast training phase, two shortcomings can be pointed in this approach: it assigns its input weights randomly and it is not able to handle sequential learning. Thereby, the online sequential extreme learning machine (OS-ELM) was proposed in order to tackle the lack of sequential learning on ELMs.
abstract: The extreme learning machine (ELM) is an analytical algorithm developed to train single-hidden layer feedforward neural network (SLFN). Although the ELM provides a good generalization and a very fast training phase, two shortcomings can be pointed in this approach: it assigns its input weights randomly and it is not able to handle sequential learning. Thereby, the online sequential extreme learning machine (OS-ELM) was proposed in order to tackle the lack of sequential learning on ELMs. Nonetheless, the OS-ELM still assigns the input weights randomly. Thus, in this work, we proposed an approach based on a restricted Boltzmann machine (RBM) to improve the OS-ELM by determining its input weights throughout the training phase. We applied our method to several well-known benchmarks and the results show that the proposed approach is able to improve the OS-ELM for most them.
key_contribution: The extreme learning machine (ELM) is an analytical algorithm developed to train single-hidden layer feedforward neural network (SLFN).
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: Adaptive Subgradient Methods for Online Learning
description: Introduced Adagrad, an adaptive learning rate optimizer that accumulates squared gradients per parameter.
abstract: We introduce AdaGrad, an adaptive gradient method that scales the learning rate of each parameter inversely with the square root of the sum of all its historical squared gradients, allowing infrequent features to receive larger updates. This per-parameter adaptation is particularly beneficial for sparse gradient settings arising in NLP and recommendation, where features occur with vastly different frequencies. We prove regret bounds that are significantly tighter than those of standard gradient descent in sparse settings. AdaGrad pioneered the family of adaptive optimization algorithms that includes RMSProp, Adam, and their variants.
key_contribution: Adaptive per-parameter learning rates for online learning; pioneered adaptive optimization methods.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Machine Learning Research

name: ImageNet Classification with Deep CNNs (AlexNet)
description: AlexNet won ILSVRC 2012 with a 10.8% gap, triggering the deep learning revolution in computer vision.
abstract: We present a large, deep convolutional neural network trained on the 1.2 million images of the ILSVRC-2012 dataset that achieves top-5 test error of 15.3%, compared to 26.2% by the second-best entry. The network consists of five convolutional layers and three fully connected layers, uses ReLU nonlinearities for faster training, dropout regularization to reduce overfitting, and is trained on two GPUs in parallel. We introduce several architectural innovations including local response normalization and overlapping max-pooling. This work launched the modern deep learning era in computer vision and demonstrated the power of large-scale supervised learning with deep networks.
key_contribution: Deep CNN with ReLU, dropout, and GPU training won ImageNet 2012 by a large margin, sparking the deep learning era.

name: ADADELTA: An Adaptive Learning Rate Method
description: Introduced ADADELTA, an adaptive learning rate method that requires no manual tuning of the global learning rate.
abstract: We propose ADADELTA, an adaptive learning rate method that requires no manual specification of a global learning rate by adapting the step size based on a running average of recent gradient magnitudes and recent update magnitudes. Unlike AdaGrad, which accumulates all past gradients and leads to monotonically shrinking learning rates, ADADELTA uses an exponentially decaying window, maintaining a non-vanishing learning rate throughout training. We provide theoretical analysis and demonstrate competitive performance with other adaptive methods on speech recognition and image classification benchmarks without any hyperparameter tuning. The method is robust to noisy gradients and scales well to large architectures.
key_contribution: Eliminated manual learning rate tuning with adaptive second-moment gradient scaling.

name: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space (Word2Vec)
description: Introduced Word2Vec, efficient neural methods for learning dense word vector representations from large text corpora.
abstract: We propose two architectures—Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW) and Skip-gram—for efficiently computing dense vector representations of words from large text corpora, using a shallow neural network trained to predict words from context or context from words. The resulting word vectors capture rich semantic and syntactic regularities, famously exhibiting algebraic relationships such as king − man + woman ≈ queen. Training on 100 billion words achieves state-of-the-art results on word analogy and similarity tasks at a fraction of the computational cost of earlier neural language models. Word2Vec popularized the paradigm of dense word embeddings that underlies all subsequent NLP representation learning.
key_contribution: Efficient shallow neural network for dense word embeddings capturing semantic and syntactic relationships.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
description: Visualization and topic modeling are widely used approaches for text analysis. Traditional visualization methods find low-dimensional representations of documents in the visualization space (typically 2D or 3D) that can be displayed using a scatterplot. In contrast, topic modeling aims to discover topics from text, but for visualization, one needs to perform a post-hoc embedding using dimensionality reduction methods.
abstract: Visualization and topic modeling are widely used approaches for text analysis. Traditional visualization methods find low-dimensional representations of documents in the visualization space (typically 2D or 3D) that can be displayed using a scatterplot. In contrast, topic modeling aims to discover topics from text, but for visualization, one needs to perform a post-hoc embedding using dimensionality reduction methods. Recent approaches propose using a generative model to jointly find topics and visualization, allowing the semantics to be infused in the visualization space for a meaningful interpretation. A major challenge that prevents these methods from being used practically is the scalability of their inference algorithms. We present, to the best of our knowledge, the first fast Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes based inference method for jointly inferring topics and visualization. Since our method is black box, it can handle model changes efficiently with little mathematical rederivation effort. We demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of our method on real-world large datasets and compare it with existing baselines.
key_contribution: Visualization and topic modeling are widely used approaches for text analysis.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
description: We present the first deep learning model to successfully learn control policies directly from high-dimensional sensory input using reinforcement learning. The model is a convolutional neural network, trained with a variant of Q-learning, whose input is raw pixels and whose output is a value function estimating future rewards. We apply our method to seven Atari 2600 games from the Arcade Learning Environment, with no adjustment of the architecture or learning algorithm.
abstract: We present the first deep learning model to successfully learn control policies directly from high-dimensional sensory input using reinforcement learning. The model is a convolutional neural network, trained with a variant of Q-learning, whose input is raw pixels and whose output is a value function estimating future rewards. We apply our method to seven Atari 2600 games from the Arcade Learning Environment, with no adjustment of the architecture or learning algorithm. We find that it outperforms all previous approaches on six of the games and surpasses a human expert on three of them.
key_contribution: We present the first deep learning model to successfully learn control policies directly from high-dimensional sensory input using reinforcement learning.

name: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
description: We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments. The method is straightforward to implement, is computationally efficient, has little memory requirements, is invariant to diagonal rescaling of the gradients, and is well suited for problems that are large in terms of data and/or parameters. The method is also appropriate for non-stationary objectives and problems with very noisy and/or sparse gradients.
abstract: We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments. The method is straightforward to implement, is computationally efficient, has little memory requirements, is invariant to diagonal rescaling of the gradients, and is well suited for problems that are large in terms of data and/or parameters. The method is also appropriate for non-stationary objectives and problems with very noisy and/or sparse gradients. The hyper-parameters have intuitive interpretations and typically require little tuning. Some connections to related algorithms, on which Adam was inspired, are discussed. We also analyze the theoretical convergence properties of the algorithm and provide a regret bound on the convergence rate that is comparable to the best known results under the online convex optimization framework. Empirical results demonstrate that Adam works well in practice and compares favorably to other stochastic optimization methods. Finally, we discuss AdaMax, a variant of Adam based on the infinity norm.
key_contribution: We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments.

name: Generative Adversarial Nets
description: Introduced Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), a framework for training generative models through an adversarial game.
abstract: We introduce a framework for training generative models via an adversarial process in which a generator network learns to produce samples that fool a discriminator network trained simultaneously to distinguish real from generated data. The generator and discriminator are both trained with standard backpropagation, with the generator minimizing and the discriminator maximizing the same value function. We prove that the global optimum corresponds to the generator recovering the true data distribution, and demonstrate the framework's ability to generate sharp, realistic images of faces, digits, and natural scenes. GANs sparked an explosion of research in deep generative modeling and have transformed image synthesis.
key_contribution: Introduced adversarial training between generator and discriminator; transformed image synthesis.

name: GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation
