description: Recent methods for learning vector space representations of words have succeeded in capturing fine-grained semantic and syntactic regularities using vector arithmetic, but the origin of these regularities has remained opaque. We analyze and make explicit the model properties needed for such regularities to emerge in word vectors. The result is a new global logbilinear regression model that combines the advantages of the two major model families in the literature: global matrix factorization and local context window methods.
abstract: Recent methods for learning vector space representations of words have succeeded in capturing fine-grained semantic and syntactic regularities using vector arithmetic, but the origin of these regularities has remained opaque. We analyze and make explicit the model properties needed for such regularities to emerge in word vectors. The result is a new global logbilinear regression model that combines the advantages of the two major model families in the literature: global matrix factorization and local context window methods. Our model efficiently leverages statistical information by training only on the nonzero elements in a word-word cooccurrence matrix, rather than on the entire sparse matrix or on individual context windows in a large corpus. The model produces a vector space with meaningful substructure, as evidenced by its performance of 75% on a recent word analogy task. It also outperforms related models on similarity tasks and named entity recognition.
key_contribution: Recent methods for learning vector space representations of words have succeeded in capturing fine-grained semantic and syntactic regularities using vector arithmetic, but the origin of these regul...
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: Convolutional Neural Networks for Sentence Classification
description: Demonstrated that simple CNNs with pre-trained word vectors achieve excellent results on sentence classification tasks.
abstract: We present a simple convolutional neural network architecture for sentence-level classification tasks, using 1D convolutions over pre-trained word vectors followed by max-over-time pooling and a softmax classifier. Despite its simplicity, the model achieves strong results across seven benchmark datasets including sentiment analysis, question type classification, and subjectivity detection. We show that fine-tuning the word vectors during training further improves performance, and that the pre-trained embeddings provide a strong regularization signal. This work demonstrated that CNNs with pre-trained word vectors are highly competitive for NLP classification, influencing the design of subsequent text classification architectures.
key_contribution: Showed simple 1D CNNs over word embeddings achieve strong NLP classification results.
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: Improving neural networks by preventing co-adaptation of feature detectors
description: We report on a series of experiments with convolutional neural networks (CNN) trained on top of pre-trained word vectors for sentence-level classification tasks. We show that a simple CNN with little hyperparameter tuning and static vectors achieves excellent results on multiple benchmarks. Learning task-specific vectors through fine-tuning offers further gains in performance.
abstract: We report on a series of experiments with convolutional neural networks (CNN) trained on top of pre-trained word vectors for sentence-level classification tasks. We show that a simple CNN with little hyperparameter tuning and static vectors achieves excellent results on multiple benchmarks. Learning task-specific vectors through fine-tuning offers further gains in performance. We additionally propose a simple modification to the architecture to allow for the use of both task-specific and static vectors. The CNN models discussed herein improve upon the state of the art on 4 out of 7 tasks, which include sentiment analysis and question classification.
key_contribution: We report on a series of experiments with convolutional neural networks (CNN) trained on top of pre-trained word vectors for sentence-level classification tasks.

name: Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks
description: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are powerful models that have achieved excellent performance on difficult learning tasks. Although DNNs work well whenever large labeled training sets are available, they cannot be used to map sequences to sequences. In this paper, we present a general end-to-end approach to sequence learning that makes minimal assumptions on the sequence structure.
abstract: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are powerful models that have achieved excellent performance on difficult learning tasks. Although DNNs work well whenever large labeled training sets are available, they cannot be used to map sequences to sequences. In this paper, we present a general end-to-end approach to sequence learning that makes minimal assumptions on the sequence structure. Our method uses a multilayered Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) to map the input sequence to a vector of a fixed dimensionality, and then another deep LSTM to decode the target sequence from the vector. Our main result is that on an English to French translation task from the WMT'14 dataset, the translations produced by the LSTM achieve a BLEU score of 34.8 on the entire test set, where the LSTM's BLEU score was penalized on out-of-vocabulary words. Additionally, the LSTM did not have difficulty on long sentences. For comparison, a phrase-based SMT system achieves a BLEU score of 33.3 on the same dataset. When we used the LSTM to rerank the 1000 hypotheses produced by the aforementioned SMT system, its BLEU score increases to 36.5, which is close to the previous best result on this task. The LSTM also learned sensible phrase and sentence representations that are sensitive to word order and are relatively invariant to the active and the passive voice. Finally, we found that reversing the order of the words in all source sentences (but not target sentences) improved the LSTM's performance markedly, because doing so introduced many short term dependencies between the source and the target sentence which made the optimization problem easier.
key_contribution: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are powerful models that have achieved excellent performance on difficult learning tasks.

name: Rich feature hierarchies for accurate object detection and semantic segmentation (R-CNN)
description: Formulation of the problem. Over the past few years, there has been little progress in object detection techniques. The most efficient are complex computational ensemble methods, which usually combine several low-level image properties with high-level properties.
abstract: Formulation of the problem. Over the past few years, there has been little progress in object detection techniques. The most efficient are complex computational ensemble methods, which usually combine several low-level image properties with high-level properties. However, every day interest in artificial intelligence is growing, and the idea of using neural networks on board a spacecraft, with the possibility of making decisions and issuing one-time commands, is very promising, since it makes it possible to analyze a large data stream in real time without resorting to ground station, thereby not losing information when transmitting a packet. The purpose of the work is to conduct research on the possibility of effective use of modern models of neural networks, to develop a methodology for their use in the problem of object detection and analysis of the element base for hardware implementation with the possibility of using convolutional neural networks for thermovideotelemetry on board a spacecraft. Results of work. An approach has been formulated that combines two key ideas: 1) application of high-throughput convolutional neural networks for downward processing of image regions in order to localize and segment objects; 2) preliminary training for the auxiliary task, followed by fine tuning of the domain, which gives a significant increase in performance in the case when the training data is insufficient. The analysis of the element base for the possibility of hardware implementation of neural networks on board a spacecraft using electrical radio products of domestic and foreign production is carried out. Practical significance. The efficiency of preliminary network training for an auxiliary task is shown, followed by fine tuning of the subject area. A technique is described that makes it possible to increase the average accuracy of detecting objects in an image by more than 30%. The analysis of the existing element base, the possibility of hardware implementation of neural networks on board the spacecraft using electrical radio products of domestic and foreign production,
key_contribution: Formulation of the problem.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition (VGGNet)
description: VGGNet showed that network depth using small 3×3 filters is critical for strong performance on ImageNet.
abstract: We investigate the effect of convolutional network depth on accuracy in large-scale image recognition, showing that networks using very small (3×3) convolutional filters can be pushed to 16–19 weight layers with substantial accuracy improvements. Replacing large-kernel convolutions with stacks of 3×3 filters increases depth while keeping the receptive field size constant and reducing the number of parameters. Our VGG nets achieve top-5 error rates of 7.3% on ILSVRC-2014 and generalize well to other datasets, demonstrating the importance of depth for feature hierarchy. VGGNet became a widely adopted backbone for transfer learning and feature extraction in computer vision.
key_contribution: Demonstrated that depth with small (3×3) convolutions is key to performance; widely used backbone.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
description: Introduces an encoder–decoder CNN with skip connections for biomedical image segmentation.
abstract: There is large consent that successful training of deep networks requires many thousands of annotated training samples. In this paper, we present a network and training strategy that relies on the strong use of data augmentation to use the available annotated samples more efficiently. The architecture consists of a contracting path to capture context and a symmetric expanding path that enables precise localization.
key_contribution: Introduces an encoder–decoder CNN with skip connections for biomedical image segmentation.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention (MICCAI)

name: Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift
description: Introduced batch normalization to reduce internal covariate shift, enabling much faster and more stable training of deep networks.
abstract: We address the problem of internal covariate shift—the change in the distribution of layer inputs during training—by introducing Batch Normalization, which normalizes each mini-batch's activations to have zero mean and unit variance, then applies learned scale and shift parameters. By reducing covariate shift, Batch Normalization allows the use of higher learning rates, acts as a regularizer reducing the need for Dropout, and makes networks far less sensitive to initialization. We achieve a 14× speedup in training of Inception networks and state-of-the-art performance on ImageNet. Batch Normalization became a universal component in modern deep learning architectures.
key_contribution: Normalizing layer inputs during training enables much faster training and acts as regularization.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection
description: The objective of the work is to increase the precision of object detection using novel image classification using machine learning algorithms. Materials and Methods: The categorising is performed by adopting a sample size of n = 10 in Faster R-CNN (RPN) and sample size n = 10 in Fast R-CNN (Selective Search) algorithms with a sample size = 10 and the G-Power analysis was carried out with 80% and confidence interval 95%. Results and Discussion: The observation of the outcomes shows that the Faster R-CNN using region proposal networks has a high accuracy of 81.72% in comparison with the Selective Search based Fast R-CNN 79.61%.
abstract: The objective of the work is to increase the precision of object detection using novel image classification using machine learning algorithms. Materials and Methods: The categorising is performed by adopting a sample size of n = 10 in Faster R-CNN (RPN) and sample size n = 10 in Fast R-CNN (Selective Search) algorithms with a sample size = 10 and the G-Power analysis was carried out with 80% and confidence interval 95%. Results and Discussion: The observation of the outcomes shows that the Faster R-CNN using region proposal networks has a high accuracy of 81.72% in comparison with the Selective Search based Fast R-CNN 79.61%. A statistically significant difference exists between the research groups with p=0.028 (2 tailed) (p<0.05). Conclusion: Detection of objects with high accuracy using machine learning algorithms shows that the regional proposal network based Faster R-CNN generates higher accuracy than the Selective search (Fast RCNN) algorithm.
key_contribution: The objective of the work is to increase the precision of object detection using novel image classification using machine learning algorithms.

name: Fully Convolutional Networks for Semantic Segmentation
description: Convolutional networks are powerful visual models that yield hierarchies of features. We show that convolutional networks by themselves, trained end-to-end, pixels-to-pixels, exceed the state-of-the-art in semantic segmentation. Our key insight is to build “fully convolutional” networks that take input of arbitrary size and produce correspondingly-sized output with efficient inference and learning.
abstract: Convolutional networks are powerful visual models that yield hierarchies of features. We show that convolutional networks by themselves, trained end-to-end, pixels-to-pixels, exceed the state-of-the-art in semantic segmentation. Our key insight is to build “fully convolutional” networks that take input of arbitrary size and produce correspondingly-sized output with efficient inference and learning. We define and detail the space of fully convolutional networks, explain their application to spatially dense prediction tasks, and draw connections to prior models. We adapt contemporary classification networks (AlexNet [20], the VGG net [31], and GoogLeNet [32]) into fully convolutional networks and transfer their learned representations by fine-tuning [3] to the segmentation task. We then define a skip architecture that combines semantic information from a deep, coarse layer with appearance information from a shallow, fine layer to produce accurate and detailed segmentations. Our fully convolutional network achieves state-of-the-art segmentation of PASCAL VOC (20% relative improvement to 62.2% mean IU on 2012), NYUDv2, and SIFT Flow, while inference takes less than one fifth of a second for a typical image.
key_contribution: Convolutional networks are powerful visual models that yield hierarchies of features.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Going Deeper with Convolutions (Inception/GoogLeNet)
description: Abstract Network in Network is an important extension of the deep convolution neural network that uses a shallow multilayer perceptron (MLP), a nonlinear function, to replace the linear filter. In this article, we propose to replace convolution layers with convolution modules. The main feature of this architecture is the improved utilization of computing resources inside the network.
abstract: Abstract Network in Network is an important extension of the deep convolution neural network that uses a shallow multilayer perceptron (MLP), a nonlinear function, to replace the linear filter. In this article, we propose to replace convolution layers with convolution modules. The main feature of this architecture is the improved utilization of computing resources inside the network. This has been achieved through a carefully crafted design that allows for increased network depth and width while keeping the compute budget constant. The experimental results on the CIFAR10 dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method
key_contribution: Abstract Network in Network is an important extension of the deep convolution neural network that uses a shallow multilayer perceptron (MLP), a nonlinear function, to replace the lin...
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Delving Deep into Rectifiers (He init)
description: Rectified activation units (rectifiers) are essential for state-of-the-art neural networks. In this work, we study rectifier neural networks for image classification from two aspects. First, we propose a Parametric Rectified Linear Unit (PReLU) that generalizes the traditional rectified unit.
abstract: Rectified activation units (rectifiers) are essential for state-of-the-art neural networks. In this work, we study rectifier neural networks for image classification from two aspects. First, we propose a Parametric Rectified Linear Unit (PReLU) that generalizes the traditional rectified unit. PReLU improves model fitting with nearly zero extra computational cost and little overfitting risk. Second, we derive a robust initialization method that particularly considers the rectifier nonlinearities. This method enables us to train extremely deep rectified models directly from scratch and to investigate deeper or wider network architectures. Based on the learnable activation and advanced initialization, we achieve 4.94% top-5 test error on the ImageNet 2012 classification dataset. This is a 26% relative improvement over the ILSVRC 2014 winner (GoogLeNet, 6.66% [33]). To our knowledge, our result is the first to surpass the reported human-level performance (5.1%, [26]) on this dataset.
key_contribution: Rectified activation units (rectifiers) are essential for state-of-the-art neural networks.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation (Luong attention)
description: An attentional mechanism has lately been used to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by selectively focusing on parts of the source sentence during translation. However, there has been little work exploring useful architectures for attention-based NMT. This paper examines two simple and effective classes of attentional mechanism: a global approach which always attends to all source words and a local one that only looks at a subset of source words at a time.
abstract: An attentional mechanism has lately been used to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by selectively focusing on parts of the source sentence during translation. However, there has been little work exploring useful architectures for attention-based NMT. This paper examines two simple and effective classes of attentional mechanism: a global approach which always attends to all source words and a local one that only looks at a subset of source words at a time. We demonstrate the effectiveness of both approaches on the WMT translation tasks between English and German in both directions. With local attention, we achieve a significant gain of 5.0 BLEU points over non-attentional systems that already incorporate known techniques such as dropout. Our ensemble model using different attention architectures yields a new state-of-the-art result in the WMT'15 English to German translation task with 25.9 BLEU points, an improvement of 1.0 BLEU points over the existing best system backed by NMT and an n-gram reranker. 1
key_contribution: An attentional mechanism has lately been used to improve neural machine translation (NMT) by selectively focusing on parts of the source sentence during translation.
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
description: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions.
abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide comprehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers - 8× deeper than VGG nets [40] but still having lower complexity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error on the ImageNet test set. This result won the 1st place on the ILSVRC 2015 classification task. We also present analysis on CIFAR-10 with 100 and 1000 layers. The depth of representations is of central importance for many visual recognition tasks. Solely due to our extremely deep representations, we obtain a 28% relative improvement on the COCO object detection dataset. Deep residual nets are foundations of our submissions to ILSVRC & COCO 2015 competitions1, where we also won the 1st places on the tasks of ImageNet detection, ImageNet localization, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation.
key_contribution: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System
description: Tree boosting is a highly effective and widely used machine learning method. In this paper, we describe a scalable end-to-end tree boosting system called XGBoost, which is used widely by data scientists to achieve state-of-the-art results on many machine learning challenges. We propose a novel sparsity-aware algorithm for sparse data and weighted quantile sketch for approximate tree learning.
abstract: Tree boosting is a highly effective and widely used machine learning method. In this paper, we describe a scalable end-to-end tree boosting system called XGBoost, which is used widely by data scientists to achieve state-of-the-art results on many machine learning challenges. We propose a novel sparsity-aware algorithm for sparse data and weighted quantile sketch for approximate tree learning. More importantly, we provide insights on cache access patterns, data compression and sharding to build a scalable tree boosting system. By combining these insights, XGBoost scales beyond billions of examples using far fewer resources than existing systems.
key_contribution: Tree boosting is a highly effective and widely used machine learning method.
peer_reviewed_by: ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining

name: SSD: Single Shot MultiBox Detector
description: Tujuan penelitian untuk mengembangkan sistem pendeteksi kalori makanan dengan menggunakan metode Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD). Juga, bertujuan untuk mengatasi masalah manusia yang kesulitan dalam mengestimasi jumlah kalori yang dikonsumsi dari makanan. Dengan menggunakan model kecerdasan buatan dan bantuan kamera pada perangkat ponsel, pada penelitian ini memungkinkan pengguna untuk melakukan estimasi kalori yang lebih akurat.
abstract: Tujuan penelitian untuk mengembangkan sistem pendeteksi kalori makanan dengan menggunakan metode Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD). Juga, bertujuan untuk mengatasi masalah manusia yang kesulitan dalam mengestimasi jumlah kalori yang dikonsumsi dari makanan. Dengan menggunakan model kecerdasan buatan dan bantuan kamera pada perangkat ponsel, pada penelitian ini memungkinkan pengguna untuk melakukan estimasi kalori yang lebih akurat. Sistem ini dirancang secara otomatis untuk mengidentifikasi dan memperkirakan jumlah kalori dalam makanan berdasarkan citra visual. Pemilihan metode SSD didasarkan pada keunggulannya dalam mendeteksi objek dengan tingkat akurasi yang tinggi dan kecepatan pengolahan yang cepat. Proses penelitian melibatkan beberapa tahap, termasuk pengumpulan dataset citra makanan, pelatihan model SSD dengan konfigurasi Hyperparameter pada 40.000 langkah, menggunakan data training sebanyak 90%, validasi 10%, dan testing 10%, serta menggunakan batch size 16 dan learning rate 0.007943453. Hasil eksperimen menunjukkan total loss sebesar 0.1670681 dan mean average precision (mAP) sebesar 65.09%. Jenis makanan berhasil dideteksi dengan baik, dan aplikasi mobile terkait mampu mengestimasi kalori makanan setelah deteksi jenis makanan. Meskipun demikian, penelitian mengidentifikasi beberapa tantangan, terutama dalam meningkatkan akurasi deteksi pada makanan dengan struktur kompleks atau variasi presentasi yang ekstensif. Temuan dari penelitian ini diharapkan dapat memberikan kontribusi penting dalam pengembangan sistem pendukung keputusan untuk pemantauan otomatis asupan kalori.
key_contribution: Tujuan penelitian untuk mengembangkan sistem pendeteksi kalori makanan dengan menggunakan metode Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD).
peer_reviewed_by: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)

name: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection (YOLO)
description: Introduced YOLO, the first real-time unified object detection system that predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities with a single network.
abstract: We present YOLO, a unified real-time object detection system that reframes detection as a single regression problem directly from image pixels to bounding box coordinates and class probabilities. A single convolutional network simultaneously predicts multiple bounding boxes and class probabilities, processing the entire image in one forward pass at 45 frames per second. Although YOLO makes more localization errors than region proposal-based methods, it is significantly faster and better at avoiding false positives on background. By reasoning globally about the full image, YOLO learns generalizable representations that perform well across diverse datasets.
key_contribution: Unified real-time object detection as a single regression problem; orders of magnitude faster than prior methods.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: LIME: Why Should I Trust You?
description: Introduced LIME, a model-agnostic technique to explain individual predictions of any classifier by locally approximating it.
abstract: We introduce LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), a technique to explain the predictions of any classifier by learning a locally faithful interpretable model around each individual prediction. LIME perturbs the input and observes the model's output to fit a sparse linear model in an interpretable feature space, providing human-understandable explanations without access to the model internals. We demonstrate LIME's utility for identifying when classifiers rely on spurious correlations and for improving trust in deployed models. The method is applicable to text, image, and tabular data, and provides practitioners with actionable insight into model behavior.
key_contribution: Model-agnostic local interpretability via locally faithful linear approximations of black-box models.
peer_reviewed_by: ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining

name: Byte Pair Encoding for Neural Machine Translation
description: Introduced Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenization, now the dominant subword tokenization strategy used in virtually all modern LLMs including GPT, BERT, and LLaMA.
abstract: We introduce a simple and effective approach to open-vocabulary neural machine translation based on byte pair encoding (BPE), which represents an open vocabulary through a fixed-size vocabulary of variable-length character sequences.
key_contribution: Subword tokenization via BPE handles rare words and enables open-vocabulary NMT; now universal in LLMs.

name: SQuAD: 100,000+ Questions for Machine Comprehension
description: Introduced the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD), a benchmark that drove progress in machine reading comprehension.
abstract: We introduce the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD), a reading comprehension dataset consisting of over 100,000 question-answer pairs crowdsourced from 536 Wikipedia articles, where the answer to every question is a span of text from the corresponding passage. The dataset's scale and diversity enable training and evaluation of machine comprehension systems that must understand natural language questions and locate answers within context. We establish human performance baselines and benchmark a logistic regression model, revealing a significant gap between human and machine performance. SQuAD became the most widely used benchmark for machine reading comprehension and drove rapid progress in the field.
key_contribution: 100k+ crowd-sourced QA pairs on Wikipedia paragraphs; became the benchmark for reading comprehension.
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
description: Identified five core technical AI safety problems — avoiding side effects, reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and distributional shift — defining the field's research agenda.
abstract: We discuss five practical research problems related to accident risk in machine learning systems. These problems are: avoiding negative side effects, avoiding reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, and robustness to distributional shift.
key_contribution: Formalized key AI safety challenges (reward hacking, safe exploration, distributional shift) for the research community.

name: Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks and tree search (AlphaGo)
description: Deep learning (DL) is playing an increasingly important role in our lives. It has already made a huge impact in areas, such as cancer diagnosis, precision medicine, self-driving cars, predictive forecasting, and speech recognition. The painstakingly handcrafted feature extractors used in traditional learning, classification, and pattern recognition systems are not scalable for large-sized data sets.
abstract: Deep learning (DL) is playing an increasingly important role in our lives. It has already made a huge impact in areas, such as cancer diagnosis, precision medicine, self-driving cars, predictive forecasting, and speech recognition. The painstakingly handcrafted feature extractors used in traditional learning, classification, and pattern recognition systems are not scalable for large-sized data sets. In many cases, depending on the problem complexity, DL can also overcome the limitations of earlier shallow networks that prevented efficient training and abstractions of hierarchical representations of multi-dimensional training data. Deep neural network (DNN) uses multiple (deep) layers of units with highly optimized algorithms and architectures. This paper reviews several optimization methods to improve the accuracy of the training and to reduce training time. We delve into the math behind training algorithms used in recent deep networks. We describe current shortcomings, enhancements, and implementations. The review also covers different types of deep architectures, such as deep convolution networks, deep residual networks, recurrent neural networks, reinforcement learning, variational autoencoders, and others.
key_contribution: Deep learning (DL) is playing an increasingly important role in our lives.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: Layer Normalization
description: Training state-of-the-art, deep neural networks is computationally expensive. One way to reduce the training time is to normalize the activities of the neurons. A recently introduced technique called batch normalization uses the distribution of the summed input to a neuron over a mini-batch of training cases to compute a mean and variance which are then used to normalize the summed input to that neuron on each training case.
abstract: Training state-of-the-art, deep neural networks is computationally expensive. One way to reduce the training time is to normalize the activities of the neurons. A recently introduced technique called batch normalization uses the distribution of the summed input to a neuron over a mini-batch of training cases to compute a mean and variance which are then used to normalize the summed input to that neuron on each training case. This significantly reduces the training time in feed-forward neural networks. However, the effect of batch normalization is dependent on the mini-batch size and it is not obvious how to apply it to recurrent neural networks. In this paper, we transpose batch normalization into layer normalization by computing the mean and variance used for normalization from all of the summed inputs to the neurons in a layer on a single training case. Like batch normalization, we also give each neuron its own adaptive bias and gain which are applied after the normalization but before the non-linearity. Unlike batch normalization, layer normalization performs exactly the same computation at training and test times. It is also straightforward to apply to recurrent neural networks by computing the normalization statistics separately at each time step. Layer normalization is very effective at stabilizing the hidden state dynamics in recurrent networks. Empirically, we show that layer normalization can substantially reduce the training time compared with previously published techniques.
key_contribution: Training state-of-the-art, deep neural networks is computationally expensive.

name: Attention Is All You Need
description: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks in an encoder-decoder configuration. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely.
abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks in an encoder-decoder configuration. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train. Our model achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, improving over the existing best results, including ensembles by over 2 BLEU. On the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, our model establishes a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8 after training for 3.5 days on eight GPUs, a small fraction of the training costs of the best models from the literature. We show that the Transformer generalizes well to other tasks by applying it successfully to English constituency parsing both with large and limited training data.
key_contribution: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks in an encoder-decoder configuration.

name: Semi-Supervised Classification with Graph Convolutional Networks
description: In recent years, Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) have been successfully applied to many graph classification problems.It has the capability to learn many types data that Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) cannot handle, such as irregular data.However, we found that GCN can not completely capture the graph structure information and especially for inference on data efficiently.In this paper, we analyze the advantages and disadvantages of several models and propose two different methods of combining models.Based on that, we propose a new model by using ensemble learning Based on GCN.This model has the ability to capture the advantages of multiple models.Finally, we conduct our experiment on several datasets, and the experimental results show that our approach is effective.

name: Mask R-CNN
description: We present a conceptually simple, flexible, and general framework for object instance segmentation. Our approach efficiently detects objects in an image while simultaneously generating a high-quality segmentation mask for each instance. The method, called Mask R-CNN, extends Faster R-CNN by adding a branch for predicting an object mask in parallel with the existing branch for bounding box recognition.
abstract: We present a conceptually simple, flexible, and general framework for object instance segmentation. Our approach efficiently detects objects in an image while simultaneously generating a high-quality segmentation mask for each instance. The method, called Mask R-CNN, extends Faster R-CNN by adding a branch for predicting an object mask in parallel with the existing branch for bounding box recognition. Mask R-CNN is simple to train and adds only a small overhead to Faster R-CNN, running at 5 fps. Moreover, Mask R-CNN is easy to generalize to other tasks, e.g., allowing us to estimate human poses in the same framework. We show top results in all three tracks of the COCO suite of challenges, including instance segmentation, bounding-box object detection, and person keypoint detection. Without tricks, Mask R-CNN outperforms all existing, single-model entries on every task, including the COCO 2016 challenge winners. We hope our simple and effective approach will serve as a solid baseline and help ease future research in instance-level recognition. Code will be made available.
key_contribution: We present a conceptually simple, flexible, and general framework for object instance segmentation.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: RetinaNet: Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection
description: Introduced Focal Loss to address class imbalance in dense object detectors, achieving state-of-the-art single-stage detection.
abstract: We identify class imbalance during training as the primary obstacle preventing one-stage object detectors from matching the accuracy of two-stage detectors, and propose the Focal Loss to address it. Focal Loss dynamically down-weights the contribution of well-classified easy negatives during training, concentrating learning on the sparse set of hard examples. Built on this loss, RetinaNet is a simple, fully convolutional one-stage detector with a Feature Pyramid Network backbone that surpasses all prior one- and two-stage detectors on COCO. Our results demonstrate that one-stage detectors can achieve accuracy competitive with state-of-the-art two-stage methods while running at faster inference speeds.
key_contribution: Focal loss down-weights easy negatives, solving class imbalance in dense detectors; enabled one-stage accuracy parity.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
description: The proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm is a promising algorithm in reinforcement learning. In this paper, we propose to add an action mask in the PPO algorithm. The mask indicates whether an action is valid or invalid for each state.
abstract: The proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm is a promising algorithm in reinforcement learning. In this paper, we propose to add an action mask in the PPO algorithm. The mask indicates whether an action is valid or invalid for each state. Simulation results show that, when compared with the original version, the proposed algorithm yields much higher return with a moderate number of training steps. Therefore, it is useful and valuable to incorporate such a mask if applicable.
key_contribution: The proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm is a promising algorithm in reinforcement learning.

name: Feature Pyramid Networks for Object Detection
description: Introduced FPN, a multi-scale feature pyramid architecture that dramatically improved object detection across scales.
abstract: We exploit the inherent multi-scale, pyramidal hierarchy of deep convolutional networks to construct feature pyramids with marginal extra cost, introducing Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN) for multi-scale object detection. FPN uses a top-down pathway with lateral connections to build a feature hierarchy that combines rich semantics from deep layers with high resolution from shallow layers. Using FPN as the backbone in a basic Faster R-CNN framework achieves state-of-the-art results on COCO object detection and segmentation benchmarks. FPN has since become a standard component in virtually all modern object detection and segmentation architectures.
key_contribution: Multi-scale feature hierarchy for detecting objects at all scales; now standard in detection architectures.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: PointNet: Deep Learning on Point Sets for 3D Classification
description: Introduced PointNet, the first deep learning architecture to directly process unordered 3D point clouds.
abstract: We present PointNet, a novel deep learning architecture that directly consumes unordered point clouds and is invariant to input permutation through the use of symmetric functions such as max-pooling. The network learns both local and global features by processing each point independently and then aggregating with a global pooling operation, optionally predicting a transformation matrix to canonicalize the input. PointNet achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results on 3D object classification, part segmentation, and semantic scene parsing benchmarks while being simple and computationally efficient. This work established the paradigm of operating directly on raw 3D point sets rather than converting to voxels or meshes.
key_contribution: First deep network operating directly on unordered 3D point sets; foundational for 3D perception.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: CycleGAN: Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation
description: Introduced CycleGAN for unpaired image-to-image translation using cycle-consistency losses.
abstract: We present CycleGAN, a method for learning image-to-image translation mappings between two visual domains without paired training examples, using cycle-consistency losses that enforce forward-backward translation to be the identity. Two generators and two discriminators are trained simultaneously: one translating from domain X to Y and another from Y back to X, with cycle-consistency loss penalizing deviations from the original image. We demonstrate compelling results on tasks such as horse↔zebra, summer↔winter, and photo↔painting style transfer. CycleGAN enabled unsupervised domain transfer and inspired a large body of work on unpaired generative image translation.
key_contribution: Unpaired image-to-image translation via cycle-consistency loss; enabled domain transfer without paired data.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: SegNet: A Deep Convolutional Encoder-Decoder Architecture
description: Introduced SegNet for efficient pixel-wise semantic segmentation using an encoder-decoder with pooling index memory.
abstract: We present SegNet, an efficient deep convolutional encoder-decoder architecture for image segmentation that stores pooling indices from the max-pooling steps of the encoder and uses them to perform non-parametric upsampling in the decoder. This approach reduces memory requirements compared to methods that copy full encoder feature maps, while preserving sharp spatial details at boundaries. SegNet is evaluated on road-scene and indoor benchmarks, achieving competitive performance at lower memory and inference-time cost than alternatives. The architecture demonstrates that pooling-index-based upsampling is a practical and effective design choice for real-time semantic segmentation.
key_contribution: Encoder-decoder with pooling index transfer for semantic segmentation; efficient with limited memory.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

name: Pix2Pix: Image-to-Image Translation with cGANs
description: Introduced Pix2Pix, a conditional GAN framework for paired image-to-image translation.
abstract: We investigate conditional adversarial networks as a general-purpose solution to image-to-image translation problems, learning a mapping from input images to output images while also learning a loss function adapted to the task. The generator is a U-Net encoder-decoder and the discriminator is a PatchGAN that classifies each N×N patch of the image as real or fake, penalizing structure at the scale of image patches. We demonstrate the method on a diverse set of tasks including semantic label-to-image, aerial photo synthesis, and edge-to-photo, achieving compelling results with the same architecture and loss. Pix2Pix established conditional GANs as a versatile framework for supervised image synthesis.
key_contribution: Conditional GAN with patch discriminator for paired image translation; showed diverse supervised image synthesis.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Shapley Values for Model Interpretation
description: Introduced SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), a unified framework for interpreting model predictions using Shapley values.
abstract: We propose using Shapley values from cooperative game theory to fairly attribute the prediction of a machine learning model to each input feature, with the feature's Shapley value representing its average marginal contribution across all possible feature coalitions. We introduce SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), which computes Shapley-based attributions efficiently for tree ensembles (TreeSHAP) and approximates them for deep learning models (DeepSHAP). SHAP satisfies desirable axioms of consistency, dummy, and efficiency that prior attribution methods violate. The framework provides a unified, theoretically grounded approach to local and global model interpretability.
key_contribution: Unified feature attribution using game-theoretic Shapley values; gold standard for model explainability.

name: LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree
description: Introduced LightGBM, a highly efficient gradient boosting framework using novel histogram-based algorithms.
abstract: Gradient boosting trees (GBT) is a popular machine learning algorithm, and there exist quite a few implementations such as XGBoost and pGBRT. Although many engineering optimizations have been adopted in these implementations, the efficiency and scalability are still unsatisfactory when the feature dimension is high and data size is large.
key_contribution: Leaf-wise gradient boosting with histogram-based binning; drastically faster GBDT on large datasets.

name: Mastering the game of Go without human knowledge (AlphaGo Zero)
description: Gomoku, also called Five in a row, is one of the earliest checkerboard games invented by humans. For a long time, it has brought countless pleasures to us. We humans, as players, also created a lot of skills in playing it.
abstract: Gomoku, also called Five in a row, is one of the earliest checkerboard games invented by humans. For a long time, it has brought countless pleasures to us. We humans, as players, also created a lot of skills in playing it. Scientists normalize and enter these skills into the computer so that the computer knows how to play Gomoku. However, the computer just plays following the pre-entered skills, it doesn’t know how to develop these skills by itself. Inspired by Google’s AlphaGo Zero, in this thesis, by combining the technologies of Monte Carlo Tree Search, Deep Neural Networks, and Reinforcement Learning, we propose a system that trains machine Gomoku players without prior human skills. These are self-evolving players that no prior knowledge is given. They develop their own skills from scratch by themselves. We have run this system for a month and half, during which time 150 different players were generated. The later these players were generated, the stronger abilities they have. During the training, beginning with zero knowledge, these players developed a row-based bottom-up strategy, followed by a column-based bottom-up strategy, and finally, a more flexible and intelligible strategy with a preference to the surrounding squares. Although even the latest players do not have strong capacities and thus couldn’t be regarded as strong AI agents, they still show the abilities to learn from the previous games. Therefore, this thesis proves that it is possible for the machine Gomoku player to evolve by itself without human knowledge. These players are on the right track, with continuous training, they would become better Gomoku players.
key_contribution: Gomoku, also called Five in a row, is one of the earliest checkerboard games invented by humans.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: Deep reinforcement learning from human preferences
description: Remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) play an indispensable role in the ongoing exploration and utilization of ocean resources due to their flexibility. Deep reinforcement learning has been used to increase the autonomy of ROV operations, so as to reduce the workload on operators and minimize human errors in operations. However, it is challenging to design a reasonable reward function for various tasks due to the complexity and uncertainty of the actual marine environment.
abstract: Remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) play an indispensable role in the ongoing exploration and utilization of ocean resources due to their flexibility. Deep reinforcement learning has been used to increase the autonomy of ROV operations, so as to reduce the workload on operators and minimize human errors in operations. However, it is challenging to design a reasonable reward function for various tasks due to the complexity and uncertainty of the actual marine environment. In this paper, we propose and implement a Preference-based Reinforcement Learning (PbRL) method for ROV path tracking, which can learn control policies from a trained reward predictor based on human preferences over provided segments of ROV’s trajectories. We evaluated our proposed PbRL method in three tasks: 2D straight line path tracking, 2D sinusoidal curve path tracking, and 3D straight line path tracking, using the BlueROV2 simulator on the Gazebo platform. Our results show consistent significant advantage of our PbRL method learning from human preferences over traditional reinforcement learning PPO algorithm from predefined reward function and GAIL algorithm learning from demonstrated trajectories. • Proposed a Preference-based Reinforcement Learning (PbRL) for ROV path tracking • Introduced the concept agent training through human preferences • Results with ROV simulator show consistent advantages of PbRL over PPO method
key_contribution: Remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) play an indispensable role in the ongoing exploration and utilization of ocean resources due to their flexibility.

name: YOLOv3: An Incremental Improvement
description: Improved YOLO with multi-scale predictions and a better backbone, remaining a practical baseline for real-time object detection.
abstract: We present YOLOv3, a series of design improvements to the YOLO detection framework that substantially boost accuracy with minimal impact on speed. Key improvements include multi-scale prediction using three detection heads at different feature map resolutions, a deeper Darknet-53 backbone with residual connections, and independent binary cross-entropy loss for each class label enabling multi-label prediction. YOLOv3 achieves 57.9 AP50 on COCO at 51 ms, significantly improving small object detection compared to YOLOv2. The design decisions reflect practical engineering intuition and remain influential in the design of modern real-time detectors.
key_contribution: Multi-scale detection with Darknet-53 backbone; improved small object detection while maintaining speed.

name: GPT: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-training
description: Introduced GPT, the first large-scale language model pre-trained with a causal language modeling objective and then fine-tuned on downstream tasks.
abstract: We demonstrate that generative pre-training of a language model on a large corpus of unlabeled text, followed by discriminative fine-tuning on labeled data for each specific task, greatly outperforms training from scratch on NLP benchmarks. The model uses a multi-layer Transformer decoder pre-trained with a language modeling objective to build a general-purpose representation, which is then adapted with minimal task-specific modifications. We achieve state-of-the-art results on nine of twelve evaluated NLP tasks including natural language inference, question answering, and semantic similarity. This work established the pre-train then fine-tune paradigm that dominates NLP.
key_contribution: Pre-training a Transformer LM then fine-tuning showed transferable language representations for many NLP tasks.

name: Squeeze-and-Excitation Networks
description: Introduced SE blocks that adaptively recalibrate channel-wise feature responses, winning ILSVRC 2017.
abstract: We introduce Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks, which explicitly model interdependencies between channels of convolutional feature maps by squeezing global spatial information into a channel descriptor via global average pooling, then recalibrating channel responses through an excitation mechanism of two fully connected layers with sigmoid gating. SE blocks can be added to any standard CNN architecture with negligible computational overhead and consistently improve performance across architectures on ImageNet. Our SE-Net won the ILSVRC 2017 image classification competition, achieving 2.25% top-5 error. The SE mechanism provides a simple, powerful form of channel-wise attention that has been widely adopted.
key_contribution: Channel-wise attention via global average pooling recalibrates feature maps; won ImageNet 2017.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: ELMo: Deep contextualized word representations
description: Introduced ELMo, contextualized word embeddings from a deep bidirectional language model, greatly improving NLP tasks.
abstract: We introduce ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models), deep contextualized word representations derived from the internal states of a deep bidirectional language model (biLM) trained on a large text corpus. Unlike static word vectors, ELMo representations are functions of the entire input sentence, naturally handling polysemy and complex syntactic phenomena through context. Adding ELMo representations to existing NLP models yields large improvements on six diverse benchmarks including question answering, textual entailment, and sentiment analysis. ELMo demonstrated that deeply contextual representations substantially outperform context-free embeddings, paving the way for BERT and subsequent pre-trained Transformer models.
key_contribution: Deep contextualized word embeddings from bidirectional language models; introduced context-dependent representations.
peer_reviewed_by: Annual Conference of the North American Chapter of the ACL (NAACL)

name: DeepLab v3+: Encoder-Decoder with Atrous Separable Convolution
description: Extended DeepLab with an encoder-decoder structure and depthwise separable convolution for semantic image segmentation.
abstract: We extend DeepLabv3 with an encoder-decoder structure in which the encoder employs Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) to capture multi-scale context and the decoder progressively refines segmentation boundaries using low-level features from the backbone. We apply depthwise separable convolution to both the ASPP and decoder modules, significantly reducing computational cost while maintaining accuracy. The model achieves 89.0% mIOU on PASCAL VOC 2012 and 45.7% mIOU on Cityscapes, establishing a new state of the art at the time. DeepLabv3+ provides an effective combination of multi-scale context capture and precise boundary recovery for semantic image segmentation.
key_contribution: Atrous convolutions with encoder-decoder for semantic segmentation; achieves strong accuracy at multiple scales.
peer_reviewed_by: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)

name: Soft Actor-Critic: Off-Policy Maximum Entropy RL
description: Introduced SAC, an off-policy actor-critic algorithm with entropy regularization for robust and sample-efficient continuous control.
abstract: We present Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), an off-policy actor-critic deep reinforcement learning algorithm based on the maximum entropy reinforcement learning framework that optimizes a stochastic policy to maximize both expected return and entropy. SAC uses two soft Q-function networks and an entropy regularization coefficient to stabilize training, with automatic temperature tuning that adjusts entropy weight during learning. The algorithm demonstrates state-of-the-art sample efficiency and stability on continuous control benchmarks, outperforming on-policy methods that require orders of magnitude more environment interactions. SAC has become the benchmark algorithm for continuous control tasks in deep RL.
key_contribution: Maximum entropy RL framework for continuous control; sample-efficient and stable off-policy training.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: CBAM: Convolutional Block Attention Module
description: Introduced CBAM, a lightweight attention module for CNNs that sequentially infers channel and spatial attention maps.
abstract: We propose the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), a lightweight attention mechanism for feed-forward convolutional neural networks that sequentially applies channel attention and spatial attention to refine feature maps along both dimensions. Channel attention exploits inter-channel relationships by applying global average and max pooling followed by a shared MLP, while spatial attention uses the channel-pooled feature maps to highlight informative spatial locations. CBAM can be integrated into any CNN architecture with negligible overhead and consistently improves classification and detection performance. Experiments on ImageNet and COCO demonstrate that CBAM provides complementary gains to SE-style channel attention by adding a spatial attention component.
key_contribution: Sequential channel + spatial attention module easily plugged into CNNs for improved representation.
peer_reviewed_by: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)

name: UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation
description: Introduced UMAP, a fast dimensionality reduction and visualization technique based on Riemannian geometry.
abstract: We present UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection), a novel dimensionality reduction technique grounded in Riemannian geometry and algebraic topology that constructs a fuzzy topological representation of the high-dimensional data and finds a low-dimensional embedding that preserves this structure as closely as possible. UMAP scales well to large datasets due to stochastic gradient descent optimization, is significantly faster than t-SNE, and better preserves global structure while maintaining competitive local neighborhood quality. We provide a theoretical framework based on category theory and demonstrate UMAP's effectiveness on diverse datasets from genomics, text, and image domains. UMAP has largely supplanted t-SNE as the preferred embedding visualization technique.
key_contribution: Uniform Manifold Approximation for fast, topology-preserving dimensionality reduction; often superior to t-SNE.

name: BigGAN: Large Scale GAN Training
description: Demonstrated that scaling GAN training to large batch sizes significantly improves image synthesis quality on ImageNet.
abstract: We demonstrate that scaling up GAN training—larger batch sizes, more channels, more training steps—combined with simple architectural modifications yields dramatic improvements in class-conditional image synthesis on ImageNet. We introduce the truncation trick, a sampling procedure that trades diversity for fidelity at inference time, and use orthogonal regularization to enable stable training at scale. BigGAN achieves an Inception Score of 166.3 and FID of 7.4 on ImageNet 128×128, dramatically surpassing prior art. Our analysis reveals interesting training dynamics including collapse modes, and we provide insights into the factors limiting GAN performance at scale.
key_contribution: Scaled GANs to class-conditional high-resolution ImageNet synthesis with truncation trick.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Progressive Growing of GANs
description: Introduced progressive growing of GANs, training from low to high resolution, enabling the first high-fidelity face generation.
abstract: We describe a new training methodology for GANs in which both the generator and discriminator are grown progressively, starting from a low resolution and adding new layers that model increasingly fine details as training proceeds. This approach speeds up training and greatly stabilizes it, allowing production of high-quality 1024×1024 images of faces that are indistinguishable from real photographs. We also propose techniques including minibatch standard deviation, equalized learning rate, and pixelwise feature normalization that further improve image quality and diversity. The progressive training technique enabled the first convincing high-resolution GAN synthesis and inspired subsequent high-resolution generation architectures.
key_contribution: Training GANs by progressively increasing resolution stabilized high-resolution image generation.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Tacotron 2: Natural TTS Synthesis
description: Introduced Tacotron 2, a neural text-to-speech system generating natural-sounding speech from text.
abstract: We describe Tacotron 2, a neural text-to-speech system that synthesizes speech directly from text by combining a sequence-to-sequence model with location-sensitive attention to generate mel spectrograms, followed by a modified WaveNet vocoder to synthesize time-domain waveforms. The system achieves a mean opinion score of 4.53 on a naturalness scale, comparable to a score of 4.58 for professionally recorded speech. The end-to-end trainable architecture learns all features of speech synthesis from data, including pronunciation, prosody, and speaker characteristics, with minimal feature engineering. Tacotron 2 established neural end-to-end TTS as the dominant paradigm for high-quality speech synthesis.
key_contribution: Neural TTS with WaveNet vocoder achieving near-human speech synthesis quality.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)

name: GLUE: General Language Understanding Evaluation
description: Introduced GLUE, a multi-task benchmark and analysis platform for NLU, catalyzing the BERT/transfer learning era.
abstract: We introduce GLUE (General Language Understanding Evaluation), a benchmark consisting of nine diverse natural language understanding tasks including textual entailment, sentiment analysis, question answering, and coreference resolution, designed to evaluate and drive progress on general and transferable NLU systems. We provide a single-number aggregate score and a diagnostic dataset that probes specific linguistic phenomena. Baselines using multi-task learning on BERT-style features demonstrate the benchmark's utility for comparing models. GLUE and its successor SuperGLUE became the standard benchmarks for evaluating pre-trained language model performance.
key_contribution: Multi-task NLU benchmark providing standardized evaluation across diverse language understanding tasks.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: CatBoost: Unbiased Boosting with Categorical Features
description: Research into machine learning and deep learning methods for image categorization has been sparked by the explosion of visual content available on digital platforms, with particular interest in domains such as food image recognition. With so many food-related images available on the internet and social media platforms, automated food image categorization is a major difficulty. Deep Convolutional Neural Network architectures offer a promising approach by leveraging pre-trained models and transfer learning to extract meaningful image representations.
abstract: Research into machine learning and deep learning methods for image categorization has been sparked by the explosion of visual content available on digital platforms, with particular interest in domains such as food image recognition. With so many food-related images available on the internet and social media platforms, automated food image categorization is a major difficulty. Deep Convolutional Neural Network architectures offer a promising approach by leveraging pre-trained models and transfer learning to extract meaningful image representations. EfficientNetB0 is an example of a pre-trained deep learning architecture that has demonstrated impressive ability in extracting high-level features from images. This study explores the effectiveness of using deep features extracted from the average pooling layer of a fine-tuned EfficientNetB0 model for the classification of complex food images using the Food-101 dataset. Subsequently, the features extracted are passed as input to a Categorical Boosting (CatBoost) classifier. To assess performance, we compare the classification accuracies of CatBoost, Support Vector Machines with various kernels, and a Random Forest classifier, all utilizing the same set of extracted features, against the accuracy of the end-to-end fine-tuned EfficientNetB0 model. Our experiments reveal that the proposed EfficientNetB0-CatBoost framework achieves the highest classification accuracy for classifying complex food images, highlighting the potential of CatBoost for efficient classification of deep features.
key_contribution: Research into machine learning and deep learning methods for image categorization has been sparked by the explosion of visual content available on digital platforms, with particular interest in dom...

name: Mastering Chess and Shogi by Self-Play with a General Reinforcement Learning Algorithm (AlphaZero)
description: The game of chess is the longest-studied domain in the history of artificial intelligence. The strongest programs are based on a combination of sophisticated search techniques, domain-specific adaptations, and handcrafted evaluation functions that have been refined by human experts over several decades. By contrast, the AlphaGo Zero program recently achieved superhuman performance in the game of Go by reinforcement learning from self-play.
abstract: The game of chess is the longest-studied domain in the history of artificial intelligence. The strongest programs are based on a combination of sophisticated search techniques, domain-specific adaptations, and handcrafted evaluation functions that have been refined by human experts over several decades. By contrast, the AlphaGo Zero program recently achieved superhuman performance in the game of Go by reinforcement learning from self-play. In this paper, we generalize this approach into a single AlphaZero algorithm that can achieve superhuman performance in many challenging games. Starting from random play and given no domain knowledge except the game rules, AlphaZero convincingly defeated a world champion program in the games of chess and shogi (Japanese chess), as well as Go.Copyright © 2018 The Authors, some rights reserved; exclusive licensee American Association for the Advancement of Science. No claim to original U.S. Government Works. PMID: 30523106
key_contribution: The game of chess is the longest-studied domain in the history of artificial intelligence.

name: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
description: Introduced BERT, which pre-trains deep bidirectional transformers using masked language modeling, transforming NLP benchmarks.
abstract: We introduce BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), a method for pre-training deep bidirectional Transformer representations by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers, using Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) as pre-training objectives. The pre-trained model can be fine-tuned with a single additional output layer for a wide range of NLP tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results on eleven benchmarks including GLUE, SQuAD, and SWAG without task-specific architectural modifications. BERT's key innovation over GPT is its bidirectionality, allowing the model to incorporate context from both directions simultaneously. BERT established the fine-tuning paradigm for NLP that all subsequent language models build on.
key_contribution: Bidirectional Transformer pre-training with masked language modeling; established the fine-tuning paradigm for NLP.
peer_reviewed_by: Annual Conference of the North American Chapter of the ACL (NAACL)

name: EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks
description: To start this chapter, we will look at some different reinforcement learning techniques that have been applied to convolutional neural networks. We’re going to look at different variants of the same basic idea of having the computer explore different neural network architectures for us. We will look at some of the research which builds up to our next neural network, EfficientNet, which was partially built using these techniques.
abstract: To start this chapter, we will look at some different reinforcement learning techniques that have been applied to convolutional neural networks. We’re going to look at different variants of the same basic idea of having the computer explore different neural network architectures for us. We will look at some of the research which builds up to our next neural network, EfficientNet, which was partially built using these techniques.
key_contribution: To start this chapter, we will look at some different reinforcement learning techniques that have been applied to convolutional neural networks.
peer_reviewed_by: ICML

name: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Approach
description: Showed that BERT was significantly undertrained and optimized key hyperparameters, substantially improving performance.
abstract: We present a replication study of BERT pre-training that carefully measures the impact of key hyperparameters and training data, finding that BERT was significantly undertrained. RoBERTa is trained with larger batch sizes, more data, longer sequences, and dynamic masking, while removing the Next Sentence Prediction objective, yielding substantial improvements over the original BERT on GLUE, SQuAD, and RACE. Our analysis demonstrates that the choice of training data size and diversity, along with training duration, are crucial factors in pre-training performance. RoBERTa serves as a stronger, more reliable baseline for the NLP community.
key_contribution: Showed BERT was significantly undertrained; improved pre-training with longer training and dynamic masking.

name: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners (GPT-2)
description: Abstract In this paper we explore the development of an oil and gas language model (LM) using an unsupervised multitask learning approach. A large language model (LLM)enables computers to understand and generate human language. The study addresses data scarcity and domain-specific language challenges, showcasing the model's performance on specific oil and gas tasks and qualitative testing.
abstract: Abstract In this paper we explore the development of an oil and gas language model (LM) using an unsupervised multitask learning approach. A large language model (LLM)enables computers to understand and generate human language. The study addresses data scarcity and domain-specific language challenges, showcasing the model's performance on specific oil and gas tasks and qualitative testing. To do that, we collected a highly diversified dataset of 33,000 documents in energy and oil and gas domains to train and benchmark our model.Our findings demonstrate that even a small model, properly finetuned on domain-specific data, outperforms larger models trained on generic corpora, highlighting the benefits of finetuning LMs in technical domains. The paper contributes to advancing natural language processing (NLP) in the oil and gas industry, emphasizing the importance of addressing domain-specific nuances and limitations for improved NLP model performance and reliability.
key_contribution: Abstract In this paper we explore the development of an oil and gas language model (LM) using an unsupervised multitask learning approach.

name: A Style-Based Generator Architecture for Generative Adversarial Networks (StyleGAN)
description: Introduced StyleGAN with a style-based generator that enables fine-grained control over image synthesis at different scales.
abstract: We propose an alternative generator architecture for GANs inspired by style transfer, which synthesizes images by progressively growing feature maps and injecting learned per-channel style vectors at each scale via Adaptive Instance Normalization (AdaIN), while adding stochastic noise to control fine-grained spatial details. The style-based generator provides intuitive, scale-specific control over image attributes, enables novel mixing and interpolation techniques, and improves quality metrics significantly over ProGAN. We also introduce the Perceptual Path Length and Linear Separability metrics for evaluating disentanglement of latent representations. StyleGAN became the dominant architecture for photorealistic face and object synthesis.
key_contribution: Style-based generator with AdaIN allows fine-grained control over image synthesis at different scales.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks
description: Modified BERT with siamese networks to derive semantically meaningful sentence embeddings suitable for semantic search.
abstract: We present Sentence-BERT (SBERT), a modification of BERT using siamese and triplet network structures to produce semantically meaningful sentence embeddings that can be compared using cosine similarity. While vanilla BERT requires a cross-encoder pass for sentence pair tasks, SBERT computes independent sentence embeddings that reduce inference time for sentence-pair tasks from 65 hours to about 5 seconds on SNLI with no loss in quality. SBERT achieves state-of-the-art performance on semantic textual similarity benchmarks and outperforms previous sentence embedding methods including InferSent and Universal Sentence Encoder. SBERT made large-scale semantic search and clustering practical using BERT-quality representations.
key_contribution: Siamese BERT for semantically meaningful sentence embeddings; enabled efficient semantic search.
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: DistilBERT: A distilled version of BERT
description: Introduced DistilBERT, a smaller, faster BERT variant distilled to 40% fewer parameters while retaining 97% of language understanding.
abstract: We present DistilBERT, a smaller, faster, cheaper language model trained by distilling BERT's knowledge into a model with 40% fewer parameters, using a combination of task-specific and language model distillation losses. The student model retains 97% of BERT's performance on GLUE while being 60% faster at inference, making it practical for deployment in resource-constrained environments. We show that language model distillation can be performed during pre-training rather than fine-tuning, transferring both linguistic and factual knowledge. DistilBERT established knowledge distillation as a practical technique for compressing large pre-trained language models.
key_contribution: Knowledge distillation of BERT to a 40% smaller model retaining 97% of performance; practical compression.

name: HRNet: Deep High-Resolution Representation Learning
description: Introduced HRNet, which maintains high-resolution representations throughout the network for human pose estimation.
abstract: We present HRNet, a new architecture that maintains high-resolution representations throughout the entire network rather than recovering them from low-resolution features. Starting from a high-resolution subnetwork, HRNet progressively adds parallel lower-resolution subnetworks and repeatedly fuses representations across scales via multi-scale fusion modules. This design enables more accurate localization than encoder-decoder approaches that discard spatial information in the encoder. HRNet achieves state-of-the-art results on human pose estimation, semantic segmentation, and object detection benchmarks, demonstrating the value of maintaining high-resolution features for position-sensitive tasks.
key_contribution: Maintains high-resolution representations throughout the network; superior for pose estimation and segmentation.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: ArcFace: Additive Angular Margin Loss for Face Recognition
description: Introduced ArcFace loss for highly discriminative face recognition features, becoming the standard for face verification.
abstract: We propose ArcFace, an additive angular margin loss for deep face recognition that adds a constant angular margin penalty m between the target class and all other classes directly in the angular space of the hypersphere. This geometric interpretation gives an exact correspondence to the geodesic distance on the hypersphere, producing highly discriminative features with clear intra-class compactness and inter-class discrepancy. ArcFace achieves state-of-the-art verification accuracy on LFW, CFP, and AgeDB, and consistently outperforms prior loss functions including CosFace and SphereFace. The simplicity and effectiveness of ArcFace made it the dominant loss function for deep face recognition.
