key_contribution: Additive angular margin loss for highly discriminative face embeddings; state-of-the-art face recognition.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: FCOS: Fully Convolutional One-Stage Object Detection
description: Introduced FCOS, an anchor-free fully convolutional object detector achieving competitive accuracy without anchor boxes.
abstract: We present FCOS, a fully convolutional one-stage object detector that performs detection in a per-pixel prediction manner, predicting a 4D vector encoding the bounding box distances from each foreground pixel to the object boundaries, along with a centerness score that suppresses low-quality detections. By eliminating anchor boxes, FCOS avoids hyperparameter choices related to anchor scales, ratios, and numbers, and resolves the ambiguity problem for overlapping ground-truth boxes via multi-level prediction on FPN. FCOS achieves 44.7% AP on COCO with no bells and whistles, demonstrating that anchor-free detectors can match anchor-based counterparts. The simplicity of FCOS made anchor-free detection a mainstream approach.
key_contribution: Fully convolutional anchor-free detector; eliminates anchor hyperparameter complexity.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: CenterNet: Objects as Points
description: Introduced CenterNet, representing each object by a single center point for efficient and accurate object detection.
abstract: We model objects as single points—the center of their bounding box—and propose CenterNet, a keypoint-based object detection framework that estimates object properties such as size, 3D location, orientation, and pose directly from the center point heatmap using a standard keypoint estimation network. The approach is simpler and end-to-end trainable without NMS post-processing, achieving 28.1 FPS at 37.4 AP on COCO with DLA-34. We extend the same framework to 3D object detection and multi-person pose estimation by regressing additional keypoints from the center. CenterNet demonstrates that objects-as-points is a flexible, high-speed detection paradigm unifying multiple perception tasks.
key_contribution: Detects objects as keypoints (centers), unifying detection, segmentation, and pose estimation.

name: OpenPose: Realtime Multi-Person 2D Pose Estimation
description: Introduced OpenPose, a real-time system for multi-person 2D pose estimation using Part Affinity Fields.
abstract: We present OpenPose, a real-time multi-person 2D pose estimation system based on Part Affinity Fields (PAFs)—a set of 2D vector fields encoding the location and orientation of limbs across the image. A multi-branch CNN simultaneously predicts confidence maps for body part locations and PAFs for part associations, which are then decoded using a greedy matching algorithm to assemble full person poses. OpenPose processes 720p images at 22 FPS on GPU and handles occlusions, truncations, and contact between people more robustly than top-down methods. It became the most widely used open-source human pose estimation library, enabling applications in activity recognition, motion capture, and HCI.
key_contribution: Real-time multi-person 2D pose estimation via part affinity fields; democratized human pose research.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

name: How Powerful are Graph Neural Networks?
description: Analyzed the expressive power of GNNs and introduced GIN (Graph Isomorphism Network) as the most expressive GNN variant.
abstract: We characterize the representational power of message-passing Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) by establishing a tight connection to the Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) graph isomorphism test. We prove that any GNN is at most as powerful as the WL test in distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs, and show that GNNs with sum-based neighborhood aggregation and MLP feature transformation achieve this upper bound, proposing the Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN) accordingly. We demonstrate that other popular aggregation choices—mean pooling, max pooling—are strictly less powerful. This theoretical analysis provides a principled foundation for designing expressive GNN architectures.
key_contribution: Theoretical analysis linking GNN expressiveness to the Weisfeiler-Leman graph isomorphism test.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Physics-Informed Neural Networks: A Deep Learning Framework for Solving Forward and Inverse Problems Involving Nonlinear Partial Differential Equations
description: Abstract We introduce physics-informed neural networks – neural networks that are trained to solve supervised learning tasks while respecting any given laws of physics described by general nonlinear partial differential equations. In this work, we present our developments in the context of solving two main classes of problems: data-driven solution and data-driven discovery of partial differential equations. Depending on the nature and arrangement of the available data, we devise two distinct types of algorithms, namely continuous time and discrete time models. The first type of models forms a new family of data-efficient spatio-temporal function approximators, while the latter type allows the use of arbitrarily accurate implicit Runge–Kutta time stepping schemes with unlimited number of stages. The effectiveness of the proposed framework is demonstrated through a collection of classical problems in fluids, quantum mechanics, reaction–diffusion systems, and the propagation of nonlinear shallow-water waves.
abstract: Abstract We introduce physics-informed neural networks – neural networks that are trained to solve supervised learning tasks while respecting any given laws of physics described by general nonlinear partial differential equations. In this work, we present our developments in the context of solving two main classes of problems: data-driven solution and data-driven discovery of partial differential equations. Depending on the nature and arrangement of the available data, we devise two distinct types of algorithms, namely continuous time and discrete time models. The first type of models forms a new family of data-efficient spatio-temporal function approximators, while the latter type allows the use of arbitrarily accurate implicit Runge–Kutta time stepping schemes with unlimited number of stages. The effectiveness of the proposed framework is demonstrated through a collection of classical problems in fluids, quantum mechanics, reaction–diffusion systems, and the propagation of nonlinear shallow-water waves.
key_contribution: Introduces physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving and constraining PDE problems with neural networks.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Computational Physics

name: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis: Finding Sparse, Trainable Neural Networks
description: Proposed the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis: dense networks contain small sparse sub-networks (winning tickets) that can be trained in isolation to match full network performance.
abstract: Neural network pruning techniques can reduce the parameter counts of trained networks by over 90%, decreasing storage requirements and improving computational performance of inference without compromising accuracy. However, current experience is that the sparse architectures produced by pruning are difficult to train from the start, which would similarly improve training performance.
key_contribution: Identified sparse subnetworks that train as well as full networks; motivated neural network pruning research.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Megatron-LM: Training Multi-Billion Parameter Language Models Using Model Parallelism
description: Large language models have led to state-of-the-art accuracies across a range of tasks. However, training these models efficiently is challenging for two reasons: a) GPU memory capacity is limited, making it impossible to fit large models on even a multi-GPU server, and b) the number of compute operations required to train these models can result in unrealistically long training times. Consequently, new methods of model parallelism such as tensor and pipeline parallelism have been proposed.
abstract: Large language models have led to state-of-the-art accuracies across a range of tasks. However, training these models efficiently is challenging for two reasons: a) GPU memory capacity is limited, making it impossible to fit large models on even a multi-GPU server, and b) the number of compute operations required to train these models can result in unrealistically long training times. Consequently, new methods of model parallelism such as tensor and pipeline parallelism have been proposed. Unfortunately, naive usage of these methods leads to fundamental scaling issues at thousands of GPUs, e.g., due to expensive cross-node communication or devices spending significant time waiting on other devices to make progress. In this paper, we show how different types of parallelism methods (tensor, pipeline, and data parallelism) can be composed to scale to thousands of GPUs and models with trillions of parameters. We survey techniques for pipeline parallelism and propose a novel interleaved pipeline parallelism schedule that can improve throughput by 10+% with memory footprint comparable to existing approaches. We quantitatively study the trade-offs between tensor, pipeline, and data parallelism, and provide intuition as to how to configure distributed training of a large model. Our approach allows us to perform training iterations on a model with 1 trillion parameters at 502 petaFLOP/s on 3072 GPUs with achieved per-GPU throughput of 52% of theoretical peak. Our code is open sourced at https://github.com/nvidia/megatron-lm.
key_contribution: Large language models have led to state-of-the-art accuracies across a range of tasks.

name: Mastering Atari, Go, Chess and Shogi by Planning with a Learned Model
description: Introduced MuZero, a model-based reinforcement learning algorithm that masters multiple domains without prior knowledge of the rules.
abstract: Constructing agents with planning capabilities has long been one of the main challenges in the pursuit of artificial intelligence. Tree-based planning methods have enjoyed great success in challenging domains, such as chess and Go, where a perfect simulator is available. However, in most real-world problems, the dynamics governing the environment are complex and unknown. Here we present the MuZero algorithm which, by learning a model that provides accurate predictions for three important quantities: the value, the policy, and the reward, achieves superhuman performance in Atari games as well as in chess, shogi, and Go.
key_contribution: Mastered Atari, Go, chess, and shogi by learning a model purely from observations without game rules.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
description: Contrastive language-audio pretraining (CLAP) has become a new paradigm to learn audio concepts with audio-text pairs. CLAP models have shown unprecedented performance as zero-shot classifiers on downstream tasks. To further adapt CLAP with domain-specific knowledge, a popular method is to finetune its audio encoder with available labelled examples.
abstract: Contrastive language-audio pretraining (CLAP) has become a new paradigm to learn audio concepts with audio-text pairs. CLAP models have shown unprecedented performance as zero-shot classifiers on downstream tasks. To further adapt CLAP with domain-specific knowledge, a popular method is to finetune its audio encoder with available labelled examples. However, this is challenging in low-shot scenarios, as the amount of annotations is limited compared to the model size. In this work, we introduce a Training-efficient (Treff) adapter to rapidly learn with a small set of examples while maintaining the capacity for zero-shot classification. First, we propose a cross-attention linear model (CALM) to map a set of labelled examples and test audio to test labels. Second, we find initialising CALM as a cosine measurement improves our Treff adapter even without training. The Treff adapter outperforms metric-based methods in few-shot settings and yields competitive results to fully-supervised methods.
key_contribution: Contrastive language-audio pretraining (CLAP) has become a new paradigm to learn audio concepts with audio-text pairs.

name: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words
description: Introduced the Vision Transformer (ViT), showing that pure transformer architectures applied directly to image patches can match or exceed CNNs at scale.
abstract: We show that a pure Transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform image recognition at competitive or superior accuracy to convolutional networks when pre-trained on large data. The image is split into fixed-size patches, each linearly embedded to a sequence of tokens, and processed by a standard Transformer encoder pre-trained with supervised classification on large datasets such as JFT-300M. Vision Transformer (ViT) achieves 88.55% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet while being more computationally efficient than large ResNets. This work demonstrated that attention-based architectures without inductive biases of convolution can match CNNs at scale, shifting the field toward Transformer-based vision models.
key_contribution: Showed pure Transformer on image patches rivals CNNs at scale; shifted CV toward attention-based architectures.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models
description: DiffuCpG 1. Introduction In this study, we used a generative AI diffusion model to address missing methylation data. We trained the model with Whole-Genome Bisulfite Sequencing data from 26 acute myeloid leukemia samples and validated it with Reduced Representation Bisulfite Sequencing data from 93 myelodysplastic syndrome and 13 normal samples.
abstract: DiffuCpG 1. Introduction In this study, we used a generative AI diffusion model to address missing methylation data. We trained the model with Whole-Genome Bisulfite Sequencing data from 26 acute myeloid leukemia samples and validated it with Reduced Representation Bisulfite Sequencing data from 93 myelodysplastic syndrome and 13 normal samples. Additional testing included data from the Illumina 450k methylation array and Single-Cell Reduced Representation Bisulfite Sequencing on HepG2 cells. Our model, DiffuCpG, outperformed previous methods by integrating a broader range of genomic features, utilizing both short- and long-range interactions without increasing input complexity. It demonstrated superior accuracy, scalability, and versatility across various tissues, diseases, and technologies, providing predictions in both binary and continuous methylation states. In this repository, we deposit the code used to build the diffusion models along with necessary example datasets to train and test a diffusion model for methylation imputation purposes. Docker Usage Install Docker Install Docker using the following link:https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/Recommended system specs: Debian 12 bookworm with 16GB RAM or more.Make sure you have the latest Nvidia GPU driver installed and docker can access your Nvidia GPU. Run Docker images with Tissue-specific Models docker pull yay135/diffucpg_tssUse our example to generate input samples with Hi-C matrix and CIS (Confidence Interval Cross Sample) data.docker run -it yay135/diffucpg_tssthenpython generate_train_test_samples.py The tissue-specific models (pytorch) are for CD34+ cells, GBM and BRCA, they are stored in folders named "model*" in the image. Run the Tissue specific modelsdocker run -it yay135/diffucpg_tssthenpython batch_run.py Run Docker images Example Models docker pull yay135/diffucpgIf you do not have a GPU enabled system, pull a CPU-only imagedocker pull yay135/diffucpg_cpuprepare your input data directory, use the following command to print a example input data directorydocker run --rm yay135/diffucpg -e trueassume your data directory name is "input_data"in windowsdocker run --gpus all -v .\input_data\:/data --rm yay135/diffucpgin unix or linuxdocker run --gpus all -v ./input_data:/data --rm yay135/diffucpg Other docker options -d or --device : select which cuda device to run with, default
key_contribution: DiffuCpG 1.

name: T5: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning
description: Introduced T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer), unifying NLP tasks as text-to-text problems and demonstrating scaling benefits.
abstract: We introduce a unified framework for NLP that treats every task as a text-to-text problem, where both input and output are always text strings, enabling the same model architecture, training procedure, and hyperparameters to be applied across diverse tasks. We conduct a large-scale empirical study exploring the effects of pre-training objectives, architectures, datasets, transfer approaches, and scale, using a new large text corpus (C4). The resulting T5 model, pre-trained with a span-corruption objective, achieves state-of-the-art results on numerous NLP benchmarks including GLUE, SuperGLUE, and SQuAD. Our study yields valuable insights into best practices for transfer learning in NLP.
key_contribution: Reframed every NLP task as text-to-text; large-scale empirical study of transfer learning approaches.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Machine Learning Research

name: BART: Denoising Sequence-to-Sequence Pre-training
description: Introduced BART, a denoising autoencoder for pre-training sequence-to-sequence models, excelling in generation tasks.
abstract: We present BART, a denoising autoencoder for pre-training sequence-to-sequence models that uses a standard Transformer architecture trained to reconstruct original text from corrupted input using arbitrary noising functions, including token masking, deletion, permutation, and text infilling. BART is particularly effective for fine-tuning on text generation tasks and matches RoBERTa on discriminative tasks while achieving new state-of-the-art results on abstractive summarization benchmarks including CNN/DM and XSum. The model generalizes several prior pre-training methods including BERT, GPT, and masked span prediction as special cases of its noising framework. BART established denoising pre-training as the dominant approach for sequence-to-sequence language models.
key_contribution: Denoising autoencoder Transformer for sequence-to-sequence tasks; excels at summarization and generation.

name: NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis
description: Introduced NeRF, a method for synthesizing novel views of complex scenes by representing them as neural radiance fields.
abstract: We represent a static scene as a continuous 5D function (3D location + 2D viewing direction) → (RGB color + volume density) parameterized by a fully connected neural network, and render novel views by marching rays through the scene and integrating radiance along each ray using classical volume rendering techniques. The network is optimized on a sparse set of input views and can synthesize photorealistic novel views of complex scenes with fine geometric and appearance detail. We introduce positional encoding to enable the network to represent high-frequency scene content and hierarchical volume sampling for efficient rendering. NeRF sparked a revolution in neural 3D scene representation and novel view synthesis.
key_contribution: Neural radiance fields synthesize novel views of scenes from 2D images via volumetric rendering.
peer_reviewed_by: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)

name: YOLOv5
description: YOLOv5 is a widely-adopted real-time object detection model with improvements in speed and accuracy over YOLOv4.
abstract: We present YOLOv5, a family of object detection architectures built on the YOLO framework with improvements including a Focus layer for efficient downsampling, a Cross Stage Partial (CSP) backbone, a Path Aggregation Network (PAN) neck, and mosaic data augmentation combining four training images. YOLOv5 provides five model sizes (nano to extra-large) trading off speed and accuracy, achieving 50.7% AP on COCO with YOLOv5x at 200 FPS on V100. The implementation emphasizes ease of use with an accessible PyTorch codebase and AutoAnchor optimization. YOLOv5 became the most widely deployed object detection model in production applications due to its combination of performance and accessibility.
key_contribution: Practical improvements to YOLO with mosaic augmentation and AutoAnchor; widely adopted in production.

name: XLM-R: Unsupervised Cross-lingual Representation Learning
description: Showed that scaling multilingual pre-training data significantly improves cross-lingual transfer learning.
abstract: We train XLM-RoBERTa (XLM-R), a multilingual masked language model on 2.5TB of filtered CommonCrawl data in 100 languages, significantly scaling up multilingual pre-training. We demonstrate that cross-lingual transfer from such a model consistently outperforms previous multilingual models including mBERT and XLM on cross-lingual NLU benchmarks, while remaining competitive with monolingual models on English-only tasks. Ablation studies reveal that more training data and larger models consistently improve cross-lingual performance, challenging the curse of multilinguality hypothesis. XLM-R provides a strong universal backbone for multilingual NLP across diverse languages and tasks.
key_contribution: Massively multilingual Transformer trained on 100 languages; strong cross-lingual transfer without parallel data.

name: Wav2Vec 2.0: A Framework for Self-Supervised Learning of Speech
description: Introduced wav2vec 2.0 for self-supervised speech representation learning, enabling speech recognition from little labeled data.
abstract: We present Wav2Vec 2.0, a framework for self-supervised representation learning of speech that masks portions of the raw audio waveform in latent space and solves a contrastive task over quantized speech representations to pre-train the model. Fine-tuning on just 10 minutes of labeled speech with Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) decoding achieves 4.8/8.2 WER on LibriSpeech clean/other, outperforming the previous best self-supervised model trained on 100× more labeled data. Large models pre-trained on LibriVox achieve 1.8/3.3 WER, approaching the performance of models trained on 960 hours. Wav2Vec 2.0 demonstrated that powerful speech representations can be learned almost entirely from unlabeled audio.
key_contribution: Self-supervised speech representation learning; enables ASR with very limited labeled audio.

name: Hugging Face Transformers Library
description: Introduced the Hugging Face Transformers library, democratizing access to pre-trained NLP models and accelerating research.
abstract: We introduce the Hugging Face Transformers library, an open-source Python library providing thousands of pre-trained Transformer models for NLP, vision, audio, and multimodal tasks in PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX. The library provides a unified API for model loading, fine-tuning, and inference, as well as Trainer utilities, tokenizers, and integration with the Model Hub for sharing pre-trained checkpoints. By abstracting architecture-specific implementation details, Transformers enables researchers and practitioners to use state-of-the-art models with minimal boilerplate. The library became the central infrastructure for NLP and multimodal AI research, with millions of downloads per month.
key_contribution: Open-source library unifying access to pre-trained Transformer models; massively accelerated NLP research.
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations (SimCLR)
description: This paper presents SimCLR: a simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations. We simplify recently proposed contrastive self-supervised learning algorithms without requiring specialized architectures or a memory bank. In order to understand what enables the contrastive prediction tasks to learn useful representations, we systematically study the major components of our framework. We show that (1) composition of data augmentations plays a critical role in defining effective predictive tasks, (2) introducing a learnable nonlinear transformation between the representation and the contrastive loss substantially improves the quality of the learned representations, and (3) contrastive learning benefits from larger batch sizes and more training steps compared to supervised learning. By combining these findings, we are able to considerably outperform previous methods for self-supervised and semi-supervised learning on ImageNet. A linear classifier trained on self-supervised representations learned by SimCLR achieves 76.5% top-1 accuracy, which is a 7% relative improvement over previous state-of-the-art, matching the performance of a supervised ResNet-50. When fine-tuned on only 1% of the labels, we achieve 85.8% top-5 accuracy, outperforming AlexNet with 100X fewer labels.
abstract: This paper presents SimCLR: a simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations. We simplify recently proposed contrastive self-supervised learning algorithms without requiring specialized architectures or a memory bank. In order to understand what enables the contrastive prediction tasks to learn useful representations, we systematically study the major components of our framework. We show that (1) composition of data augmentations plays a critical role in defining effective predictive tasks, (2) introducing a learnable nonlinear transformation between the representation and the contrastive loss substantially improves the quality of the learned representations, and (3) contrastive learning benefits from larger batch sizes and more training steps compared to supervised learning. By combining these findings, we are able to considerably outperform previous methods for self-supervised and semi-supervised learning on ImageNet. A linear classifier trained on self-supervised representations learned by SimCLR achieves 76.5% top-1 accuracy, which is a 7% relative improvement over previous state-of-the-art, matching the performance of a supervised ResNet-50. When fine-tuned on only 1% of the labels, we achieve 85.8% top-5 accuracy, outperforming AlexNet with 100X fewer labels.
key_contribution: Introduces SimCLR, a simple contrastive framework for self-supervised visual representation learning.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning

name: DETR: End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers
description: Introduced DETR, eliminating hand-designed components like NMS by reformulating object detection as a set prediction problem.
abstract: We present DETR (DEtection TRansformer), an end-to-end object detection framework that views detection as a set prediction problem and eliminates hand-designed components such as anchor generation and non-maximum suppression. DETR encodes image features with a CNN backbone and feeds them to a Transformer encoder-decoder, which directly outputs a fixed-size set of predictions matched to ground truth via Hungarian matching during training. The architecture achieves comparable accuracy to Faster R-CNN on COCO with a simpler training pipeline, and significantly outperforms it on large objects. DETR established the Transformer as a viable end-to-end alternative to anchor-based detection pipelines.
key_contribution: End-to-end object detection with Transformers via bipartite matching; removed hand-designed NMS.
peer_reviewed_by: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)

name: Open Domain Question Answering with DPR
description: Introduced Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) for open-domain QA, showing dense retrieval outperforms sparse TF-IDF-based methods.
abstract: We present Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR), a framework for open-domain question answering that uses dual dense encoders—one for questions, one for passages—trained with in-batch negatives to embed text into a shared embedding space where relevant passages are ranked by inner product similarity. DPR significantly outperforms traditional BM25 sparse retrieval on multiple open-domain QA datasets including Natural Questions, TriviaQA, and WebQuestions. When combined with a reader model, the full retrieval-augmented system achieves state-of-the-art end-to-end QA performance. DPR established dense retrieval as the foundation for modern open-domain QA and retrieval-augmented generation systems.
key_contribution: Dense embeddings for open-domain QA retrieval; replaced sparse BM25 for neural QA pipelines.
peer_reviewed_by: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP)

name: DDIM: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models
description: Introduced DDIM, enabling faster sampling from diffusion models without retraining, reducing sampling steps by 10-50x.
abstract: We present Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM), a class of iterative implicit probabilistic models with the same training procedure as DDPMs but a non-Markovian diffusion process that enables much faster sampling. By generalizing the diffusion process, we derive a family of samplers parameterized by a single scalar that interpolates between stochastic and deterministic sampling, with the deterministic limit performing 10–50× fewer function evaluations than DDPMs with comparable image quality. DDIM's deterministic sampling also enables a semantically meaningful latent space with consistent inversion properties. This work made diffusion model sampling practical and enabled latent space manipulation of diffusion-generated images.
key_contribution: Deterministic sampling for diffusion models enabling fast high-quality image generation.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
description: Introduction Many users of chatbots such as ChatGPT, have encountered the problem of receiving inappropriate or incompatible responses. There are several reasons why this might happen. One reason is the lack of appropriate training data, as chatbots are usually trained on large amounts of text and code.
abstract: Introduction Many users of chatbots such as ChatGPT, have encountered the problem of receiving inappropriate or incompatible responses. There are several reasons why this might happen. One reason is the lack of appropriate training data, as chatbots are usually trained on large amounts of text and code. If the data is insufficient or of poor quality, the chatbot may misunderstand queries and provide inaccurate responses.
key_contribution: Introduction Many users of chatbots such as ChatGPT, have encountered the problem of receiving inappropriate or incompatible responses.

name: Bootstrap Your Own Latent: A New Approach to Self-Supervised Learning
description: We introduce Bootstrap Your Own Latent (BYOL), a new approach to self-supervised image representation learning. BYOL relies on two neural networks, referred to as online and target networks, that interact and learn from each other. From an augmented view of an image, we train the online network to predict the target network representation of the same image under a different augmented view. At the same time, we update the target network with a slow-moving average of the online network. While state-of-the art methods rely on negative pairs, BYOL achieves a new state of the art without them. BYOL reaches $74.3\%$ top-1 classification accuracy on ImageNet using a linear evaluation with a ResNet-50 architecture and $79.6\%$ with a larger ResNet. We show that BYOL performs on par or better than the current state of the art on both transfer and semi-supervised benchmarks. Our implementation and pretrained models are given on GitHub.
abstract: We introduce Bootstrap Your Own Latent (BYOL), a new approach to self-supervised image representation learning. BYOL relies on two neural networks, referred to as online and target networks, that interact and learn from each other. From an augmented view of an image, we train the online network to predict the target network representation of the same image under a different augmented view. At the same time, we update the target network with a slow-moving average of the online network. While state-of-the art methods rely on negative pairs, BYOL achieves a new state of the art without them. BYOL reaches $74.3\%$ top-1 classification accuracy on ImageNet using a linear evaluation with a ResNet-50 architecture and $79.6\%$ with a larger ResNet. We show that BYOL performs on par or better than the current state of the art on both transfer and semi-supervised benchmarks. Our implementation and pretrained models are given on GitHub.
key_contribution: Introduces BYOL, self-supervised learning without negative pairs.

name: CLIP: Learning Transferable Visual Models from Natural Language
description: Introduced CLIP, which learns visual representations from natural language supervision via contrastive pre-training on 400M image-text pairs.
abstract: We train CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) on 400 million image-text pairs collected from the internet using a contrastive objective that maximizes the cosine similarity of matching image-text pairs relative to non-matching pairs in a batch. The pre-trained model achieves zero-shot transfer to 30 vision benchmarks by matching image embeddings to text prompts for each class, reaching 76.2% zero-shot accuracy on ImageNet—matching a supervised ResNet-50 without using any ImageNet training examples. CLIP's joint embedding space enables flexible vision-language tasks including zero-shot classification, image-text retrieval, and as a vision backbone for multimodal models. CLIP transformed the field by enabling natural language supervision of visual representations.
key_contribution: Contrastive image-text pre-training enabling zero-shot visual recognition via natural language prompts.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer
description: Introduced the Swin Transformer with shifted windows, achieving strong performance across vision tasks with linear complexity.
abstract: We present Swin Transformer, a hierarchical Vision Transformer that computes self-attention within shifted local windows, yielding linear computational complexity with respect to image size while allowing cross-window connections through window shifting. The architecture produces hierarchical feature maps at multiple scales, making it suitable as a general-purpose backbone for both recognition and dense prediction tasks. Swin Transformer achieves 87.3% top-1 on ImageNet-1K and state-of-the-art performance on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation. The shifted window design reconciles the efficiency of local computation with the modeling power of attention, establishing Swin as the dominant vision backbone.
key_contribution: Hierarchical Vision Transformer with shifted windows; achieves strong results across detection and segmentation.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold
description: Proteins are essential to life, and understanding their structure can facilitate a mechanistic understanding of their function. Through an enormous experimental effort 1-4 , the structures of around 100,000 unique proteins have been determined 5 , but this represents a small fraction of the billions of known protein sequences 6,7 . Structural coverage is bottlenecked by the months to years of painstaking effort required to determine a single protein structure. Accurate computational approaches are needed to address this gap and to enable large-scale structural bioinformatics. Predicting the three-dimensional structure that a protein will adopt based solely on its amino acid sequence-the structure prediction component of the 'protein folding problem' 8 -has been an important open research problem for more than 50 years 9 . Despite recent progress 10-14 , existing methods fall far short of atomic accuracy, especially when no homologous structure is available. Here we provide the first computational method that can regularly predict protein structures with atomic accuracy even in cases in which no similar structure is known. We validated an entirely redesigned version of our neural network-based model, AlphaFold, in the challenging 14th Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP14) 15 , demonstrating accuracy competitive with experimental structures in a majority of cases and greatly outperforming other methods. Underpinning the latest version of AlphaFold is a novel machine learning approach that incorporates physical and biological knowledge about protein structure, leveraging multi-sequence alignments, into the design of the deep learning algorithm.
abstract: Proteins are essential to life, and understanding their structure can facilitate a mechanistic understanding of their function. Through an enormous experimental effort 1-4 , the structures of around 100,000 unique proteins have been determined 5 , but this represents a small fraction of the billions of known protein sequences 6,7 . Structural coverage is bottlenecked by the months to years of painstaking effort required to determine a single protein structure. Accurate computational approaches are needed to address this gap and to enable large-scale structural bioinformatics. Predicting the three-dimensional structure that a protein will adopt based solely on its amino acid sequence-the structure prediction component of the 'protein folding problem' 8 -has been an important open research problem for more than 50 years 9 . Despite recent progress 10-14 , existing methods fall far short of atomic accuracy, especially when no homologous structure is available. Here we provide the first computational method that can regularly predict protein structures with atomic accuracy even in cases in which no similar structure is known. We validated an entirely redesigned version of our neural network-based model, AlphaFold, in the challenging 14th Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP14) 15 , demonstrating accuracy competitive with experimental structures in a majority of cases and greatly outperforming other methods. Underpinning the latest version of AlphaFold is a novel machine learning approach that incorporates physical and biological knowledge about protein structure, leveraging multi-sequence alignments, into the design of the deep learning algorithm.
key_contribution: Introduces AlphaFold2, achieving highly accurate protein structure prediction and transforming computational biology.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: AlphaFold: Protein Structure Prediction
description: AlphaFold 2 achieved near-experimental accuracy in protein structure prediction, representing a landmark breakthrough in computational biology.
abstract: We present AlphaFold's first-generation system for protein structure prediction, which uses an attention-based neural network combining multiple sequence alignments, evolutionary co-variation information, and distance geometry predictions to produce 3D backbone coordinates. The system won CASP13 by a large margin, demonstrating that deep learning can extract structural constraints from evolutionary sequence variation more effectively than traditional physics-based methods. We show that accurate prediction of pairwise residue distances enables high-quality 3D structure reconstruction via gradient-based optimization. AlphaFold's CASP13 results demonstrated that the protein structure prediction problem was tractable with deep learning methods.
key_contribution: Predicted protein structures using evolutionary coupling and gradient-based optimization.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: AlphaFold2 Architecture Details
description: Detailed description of the AlphaFold 2 architecture including the Evoformer module and structure module.
abstract: We describe AlphaFold2, which achieves unprecedented accuracy in protein structure prediction by introducing the Evoformer architecture—a novel Transformer that operates on 2D residue pair representations and 1D multiple sequence alignment representations simultaneously, propagating information between them through row and column attention. The Evoformer's output feeds a Structure Module that directly updates 3D backbone frames via invariant point attention, producing all-atom coordinates in a single end-to-end differentiable network trained with recycling. AlphaFold2 won CASP14 with median backbone accuracy of 0.96Å Cα RMSD, effectively solving the protein structure prediction problem for single domains. The model's predictions have been computed for virtually all known protein sequences, revolutionizing structural biology.
key_contribution: End-to-end differentiable protein structure prediction using attention over residue pairs; solved the protein folding problem.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: Diffusion Beats GANs on Image Synthesis (ADM)
description: Demonstrated that diffusion models can achieve better sample quality than GANs by introducing classifier guidance.
abstract: We show that diffusion probabilistic models can achieve image sample quality superior to state-of-the-art generative adversarial networks when given sufficient model capacity and classifier-based guidance. We improve diffusion model architecture through ablations of architecture choices including attention resolution, number of heads, and BigGAN residual blocks, and introduce classifier guidance—using gradients of a classifier trained on noisy images to steer the diffusion sampling toward desired classes. With classifier guidance, our Ablated Diffusion Model (ADM) achieves FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 256×256, surpassing BigGAN. This work established diffusion models as the dominant paradigm for high-quality image generation.
key_contribution: Showed denoising diffusion models surpass GANs on image quality when given sufficient compute and guidance.

name: DINO: Self-Supervised Vision Transformers (Self-DIstillation with NO labels)
description: Introduced DINO for self-supervised vision transformers, showing emergent segmentation and classification without labels.
abstract: We present DINO, a self-supervised training method for Vision Transformers using self-distillation with no labels, where a student network is trained to match the output of a momentum teacher network on augmented views of the same image using a cross-entropy loss with centering and sharpening. When trained with ViT, DINO features exhibit interesting properties including the emergence of segmentation structure in self-attention maps without any supervision and a nearest-neighbor classifier achieving 78.3% top-1 on ImageNet. The method requires no contrastive loss, negative pairs, or predictor network. DINO demonstrated that self-supervised ViTs produce rich, transferable visual features competitive with supervised representations.
key_contribution: Self-supervised ViTs via self-distillation produce strong segmentation and dense features without labels.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: Codex: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code (HumanEval)
description: Introduced HumanEval, a benchmark of 164 handwritten programming problems to evaluate code generation by LLMs.
abstract: We introduce Codex, a GPT language model fine-tuned on publicly available code from GitHub, and HumanEval, a benchmark of 164 original programming problems designed to evaluate functional correctness by executing generated code against test cases. Codex-12B solves 28.8% of HumanEval problems using a single sample (pass@1), rising to 70.2% using 100 samples (pass@100). We analyze the model's strengths and limitations across problem types and discuss implications for programmer productivity and AI-generated code safety. Codex forms the basis of GitHub Copilot and established code generation as a central capability of large language models.
key_contribution: LLM fine-tuned on code achieving significant pass@k on programming benchmarks; basis of GitHub Copilot.

name: GSM8K: Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems
description: Introduced GSM8K, a dataset of 8.5K grade school math problems, and showed verifiers improve LLM math reasoning.
abstract: We introduce GSM8K, a dataset of 8,500 high-quality linguistically diverse grade school math word problems requiring 2–8 step solutions, designed to test multi-step mathematical reasoning in language models. We propose training verifiers—classifiers that evaluate candidate solutions—as an alternative to fine-tuning the generator, showing that verifiers substantially outperform fine-tuning alone by selecting the best solution from a set of candidates. Using a GPT-3-scale model with verifiers, we achieve 55% accuracy compared to 35% with fine-tuning only. GSM8K became the standard benchmark for mathematical reasoning in LLMs and motivated chain-of-thought prompting research.
key_contribution: Grade school math benchmark revealing multi-step reasoning deficits in LLMs; motivated chain-of-thought work.

name: Informer: Beyond Efficient Transformer for Long Sequence Forecasting
description: Introduced the Informer architecture with ProbSparse attention for efficient long-sequence time series forecasting.
abstract: We present Informer, an efficient Transformer-based model for long sequence time-series forecasting that addresses the quadratic time and memory complexity of standard self-attention through ProbSparse self-attention, which selects a dominant set of queries based on a KL divergence measure to approximate the attention map in O(L log L) time. A distilling operation between encoder layers further reduces the memory footprint, and a generative decoder predicts long output sequences in one forward pass. Informer achieves state-of-the-art results on five large-scale long-sequence forecasting datasets. This work established the Transformer as a competitive architecture for multivariate time series forecasting.
key_contribution: Efficient Transformer for long-sequence time-series via ProbSparse self-attention.
peer_reviewed_by: AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence

name: Score-Based Generative Modeling through SDEs
description: Unified score matching and diffusion models under a stochastic differential equations framework.
abstract: We present a unified framework for score-based generative modeling based on stochastic differential equations (SDEs), showing that diffusion models and score-matching methods can be viewed as discretizations of continuous-time SDEs that gradually perturb data to noise. By reversing these SDEs using the score function, we obtain general-purpose generative models that achieve state-of-the-art sample quality. The continuous framework enables novel samplers including predictor-corrector methods, probability flow ODEs for deterministic sampling, and exact likelihood computation. This unification connects DDPM, NCSN, and score matching under a single mathematical framework and provides principled tools for designing new generative models.
key_contribution: Unified diffusion and score matching under stochastic differential equations, enabling flexible generative modeling.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: MMLU: Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding
description: Introduced MMLU, a comprehensive benchmark covering 57 subjects to measure LLM knowledge across diverse domains.
abstract: We introduce MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding), a benchmark consisting of 15,908 multiple-choice questions spanning 57 subjects from elementary mathematics to professional medicine and law, designed to measure knowledge acquired during pre-training. We evaluate GPT-3 and other models in zero-shot and few-shot settings, finding that even the best model achieves only 43.9% accuracy, barely above random chance on many subjects. Human expert performance ranges from 89.8% to 99.8% depending on subject. MMLU revealed a large gap between LLM knowledge and human expert knowledge and became the primary benchmark for measuring the breadth of LLM capabilities.
key_contribution: Massive multitask evaluation across 57 academic subjects; measures broad factual and reasoning knowledge in LLMs.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR)

name: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
description: A landmark critique of large language models examining their environmental costs, data biases, and societal harms, raising fundamental questions about the direction of NLP research.
abstract: We discuss how the field's drive to ever-larger LMs, combined with the corresponding energy costs and deployment of such models without proper consideration of the harms they can cause, represents a serious risk to society.
key_contribution: Critiqued LLM scaling on ethical, environmental, and linguistic grounds; catalyzed responsible AI discourse.
primary_venue_name: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT)

name: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models (Stable Diffusion)
description: Proposed training diffusion models in a compressed latent space rather than pixel space, enabling high-resolution synthesis at reduced compute — the basis of Stable Diffusion.
abstract: We introduce Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), which apply diffusion model training in the latent space of a powerful pre-trained autoencoder rather than pixel space, dramatically reducing the computational requirements while maintaining high synthesis quality. By working in a compressed semantic latent space, LDMs can synthesize high-resolution images 2–4× more efficiently than pixel-space diffusion models. We introduce cross-attention conditioning to enable flexible multi-modal control inputs including text, semantic maps, and other modalities. LDMs achieve state-of-the-art sample quality on text-to-image, layout-to-image, and semantic synthesis benchmarks, and form the basis of Stable Diffusion, the most widely deployed open-source image generation system.
key_contribution: Diffusion in VAE latent space enables high-resolution synthesis at practical cost; democratized text-to-image.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
description: An important paradigm of natural language processing consists of large-scale pre-training on general domain data and adaptation to particular tasks or domains. As we pre-train larger models, full fine-tuning, which retrains all model parameters, becomes less feasible. Using GPT-3 175B as an example -- deploying independent instances of fine-tuned models, each with 175B parameters, is prohibitively expensive.
abstract: An important paradigm of natural language processing consists of large-scale pre-training on general domain data and adaptation to particular tasks or domains. As we pre-train larger models, full fine-tuning, which retrains all model parameters, becomes less feasible. Using GPT-3 175B as an example -- deploying independent instances of fine-tuned models, each with 175B parameters, is prohibitively expensive. We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks. Compared to GPT-3 175B fine-tuned with Adam, LoRA can reduce the number of trainable parameters by 10,000 times and the GPU memory requirement by 3 times. LoRA performs on-par or better than fine-tuning in model quality on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3, despite having fewer trainable parameters, a higher training throughput, and, unlike adapters, no additional inference latency. We also provide an empirical investigation into rank-deficiency in language model adaptation, which sheds light on the efficacy of LoRA. We release a package that facilitates the integration of LoRA with PyTorch models and provide our implementations and model checkpoints for RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and GPT-2 at https://github.com/microsoft/LoRA.
key_contribution: An important paradigm of natural language processing consists of large-scale pre-training on general domain data and adaptation to particular tasks or domains.

name: Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners
description: This paper shows that masked autoencoders (MAE) are scalable self-supervised learners for computer vision. Our MAE approach is simple: we mask random patches of the input image and reconstruct the missing pixels. It is based on two core designs. First, we develop an asymmetric encoder-decoder architecture, with an encoder that operates only on the visible subset of patches (without mask tokens), along with a lightweight decoder that reconstructs the original image from the latent representation and mask tokens. Second, we find that masking a high proportion of the input image, e.g., 75%, yields a nontrivial and meaningful self-supervisory task. Coupling these two designs enables us to train large models efficiently and effectively: we accelerate training (by 3x or more) and improve accuracy. Our scalable approach allows for learning high-capacity models that generalize well: e.g., a vanilla ViT-Huge model achieves the best accuracy (87.8%) among methods that use only ImageNet-1K data. Transfer performance in downstream tasks outperforms supervised pre-training and shows promising scaling behavior.
abstract: This paper shows that masked autoencoders (MAE) are scalable self-supervised learners for computer vision. Our MAE approach is simple: we mask random patches of the input image and reconstruct the missing pixels. It is based on two core designs. First, we develop an asymmetric encoder-decoder architecture, with an encoder that operates only on the visible subset of patches (without mask tokens), along with a lightweight decoder that reconstructs the original image from the latent representation and mask tokens. Second, we find that masking a high proportion of the input image, e.g., 75%, yields a nontrivial and meaningful self-supervisory task. Coupling these two designs enables us to train large models efficiently and effectively: we accelerate training (by 3x or more) and improve accuracy. Our scalable approach allows for learning high-capacity models that generalize well: e.g., a vanilla ViT-Huge model achieves the best accuracy (87.8%) among methods that use only ImageNet-1K data. Transfer performance in downstream tasks outperforms supervised pre-training and shows promising scaling behavior.
key_contribution: Introduces MAE, masked autoencoders for scalable self-supervised vision learning.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback (InstructGPT)
description: Demonstrated RLHF aligns language models to follow instructions safely and helpfully, the basis for ChatGPT.
abstract: We fine-tune GPT-3 to follow a diverse set of instructions using reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), collecting human demonstrations and preference labels to train a reward model, which guides policy optimization via Proximal Policy Optimization. InstructGPT models are preferred to GPT-3 by labelers on the vast majority of prompts, with the 1.3B InstructGPT model being preferred to 175B GPT-3, despite being 100× smaller. InstructGPT also shows improvements in truthfulness and reductions in toxic outputs compared to GPT-3. This work established RLHF as the primary technique for aligning LLMs to human intent, forming the training backbone of ChatGPT.
key_contribution: RLHF with human preference data fine-tuning aligns LLM outputs to human intent; basis of ChatGPT.

name: ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue
description: Introduced ChatGPT, an RLHF-trained conversational AI that achieved unprecedented public adoption and accelerated the LLM era.
abstract: We introduce ChatGPT, a conversational language model fine-tuned from GPT-3.5 using Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), specifically optimized for multi-turn dialogue. Human trainers provided both example conversations and preference judgments between model responses, which were used to train a reward model guiding Proximal Policy Optimization. ChatGPT demonstrates broad instruction-following capabilities across coding, writing, question answering, and reasoning tasks while maintaining safety guardrails that reduce harmful outputs. Its public release in November 2022 triggered widespread public adoption and sparked a global conversation about the capabilities and risks of large language models.
key_contribution: Conversational RLHF-tuned LLM achieving broad task-following; triggered widespread public adoption of AI.

name: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
description: Showed that prompting LLMs with step-by-step reasoning examples dramatically improves performance on complex reasoning tasks.
abstract: We introduce chain-of-thought prompting, a technique that encourages large language models to produce intermediate reasoning steps before giving a final answer, by providing a few-shot exemplar demonstrations that include step-by-step solutions. We show that chain-of-thought prompting substantially improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks, with gains increasing with model scale—emerging most strongly in models above 100B parameters. On the GSM8K math benchmark, a 540B PaLM model with chain-of-thought prompting achieves 56.9%, surpassing fine-tuned GPT-3. Chain-of-thought prompting demonstrated that reasoning can be elicited from LLMs through simple prompting without additional training.
key_contribution: Elicits step-by-step reasoning in LLMs via few-shot examples with rationales; dramatically improves math and reasoning.

name: DALL-E 2: Hierarchical Text-Conditional Image Generation
description: Introduced DALL-E 2, using CLIP embeddings and cascaded diffusion for higher quality and more diverse image generation.
abstract: We present DALL-E 2, a hierarchical text-to-image generation system that first generates a CLIP image embedding from a text caption using a prior model, then decodes the image from this embedding using a diffusion model conditioned on CLIP embeddings. The system achieves a FID of 10.39 on MS-COCO zero-shot, significantly outperforming DALL-E 1 in both photorealism and semantic fidelity. The CLIP embedding space enables image variations, text-guided image editing, and inpainting by manipulating embeddings before decoding. DALL-E 2 demonstrated the power of combining CLIP guidance with diffusion models for controllable, high-fidelity text-to-image synthesis.
key_contribution: Hierarchical CLIP-guided diffusion for high-fidelity text-to-image; demonstrated fine-grained compositional control.

name: ConvNeXt: A ConvNet for the 2020s
description: Modernized ConvNets by incorporating design choices from Vision Transformers, achieving competitive performance with ViTs.
abstract: We modernize a standard ResNet by incorporating design choices inspired by Vision Transformers—including larger kernel sizes, inverted bottleneck design, separate downsampling layers, Layer Normalization, GELU activations, and fewer activation/normalization layers—yielding ConvNeXt, a pure convolutional network that matches or exceeds Swin Transformer performance across multiple tasks. ConvNeXt-L achieves 87.8% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-22K and 55.2% mAP on COCO, competitive with the best hierarchical Transformers. Crucially, these gains come without attention mechanisms, relying entirely on optimized convolutional design choices. ConvNeXt demonstrated that CNNs remain highly competitive with Transformers when modernized appropriately.
key_contribution: Modernized ResNet using Transformer design principles; showed CNNs remain competitive with ViTs.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: BLIP: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training
description: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently received substantial interest, which shows their emerging potential as general-purpose models for various vision-language tasks. MLLMs involve significant external knowledge within their parameters; however, it is challenging to continually update these models with the latest knowledge, which involves huge computational costs and poor interpretability. Retrieval augmentation techniques have proven to be effective plugins for both LLMs and MLLMs.
abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently received substantial interest, which shows their emerging potential as general-purpose models for various vision-language tasks. MLLMs involve significant external knowledge within their parameters; however, it is challenging to continually update these models with the latest knowledge, which involves huge computational costs and poor interpretability. Retrieval augmentation techniques have proven to be effective plugins for both LLMs and MLLMs. In this study, we propose multimodal adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training ( RA-BLIP ), a novel retrieval-augmented framework for various MLLMs. We first leverage the question to instruct the extraction of visual information through interactions with one set of learnable queries, minimizing irrelevant interference and redundancy during retrieval and generation. Besides, we introduce a pre-trained multimodal adaptive fusion module to achieve question text-to-multimodal retrieval and integration of multimodal knowledge by projecting visual and language modalities into a unified semantic space. Furthermore, we present an Adaptive Selection Knowledge Generation (ASKG) strategy to train the generator to autonomously discern the relevance of retrieved knowledge, which realizes excellent denoising performance. Extensive experiments on open multimodal question-answering datasets demonstrate that RA-BLIP achieves significant performance and surpasses the state-of-the-art retrieval-augmented models.
key_contribution: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently received substantial interest, which shows their emerging potential as general-purpose models for various vision-language tasks.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: Imagen: Photorealistic Text-to-Image Diffusion
description: Introduced Imagen, a photorealistic text-to-image model leveraging large LM text encoders and cascaded diffusion models.
abstract: We present Imagen, a text-to-image diffusion model that uses a large frozen T5-XXL text encoder to encode input text, and a cascade of diffusion models to generate 1024×1024 images from the text embeddings via progressive upsampling. We find that scaling the language model is more important than scaling the image diffusion model, and that classifier-free guidance is critical for sample fidelity. Imagen achieves state-of-the-art FID of 7.27 on MS-COCO zero-shot, and human raters prefer Imagen over DALL-E 2 and Stable Diffusion on photorealism and prompt alignment. We also introduce DrawBench, a comprehensive text-to-image evaluation benchmark.
key_contribution: Cascaded diffusion with large frozen T5 text encoder; demonstrated text encoder strength over CLIP for T2I.

name: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
description: Identified and analyzed emergent abilities — capabilities that appear only above certain model scales — in large language models.
abstract: We investigate emergent abilities of large language models—capabilities that are absent in smaller models but arise unpredictably as scale increases. We document dozens of emergent abilities observed in models such as GPT-4, PaLM, and Chinchilla, spanning tasks like arithmetic, multi-step reasoning, and theory of mind that show sharp phase-transition-like improvements at scale. The emergence threshold varies considerably across tasks and cannot be predicted from performance on simpler tasks. We discuss the implications of emergence for model evaluation, safety, and the scientific understanding of large language models, noting that emergence challenges smooth extrapolation of capabilities from smaller-scale experiments.
key_contribution: Documented capabilities that appear unpredictably at scale; reframed LLM scaling as qualitative phase transitions.
peer_reviewed_by: Transactions on Machine Learning Research

name: Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models
description: Introduced Switch Transformers using sparse Mixture of Experts routing to scale language models to trillion parameters efficiently.
abstract: We introduce Switch Transformers, a sparsely-activated expert model that routes each token to a single expert per layer via a learned router, replacing the dense feed-forward sublayer in the Transformer with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layer containing hundreds to thousands of expert networks. By routing each token to exactly one expert (top-1 routing), we simplify MoE design, improve training stability through a auxiliary load-balancing loss, and scale to over a trillion parameters. Switch-C, with 1.6 trillion parameters, achieves a 7× speedup over T5-XXL on pre-training perplexity with the same compute budget. Switch Transformers demonstrated the scalability of sparse mixture-of-experts models for language modeling.
key_contribution: Replaced FFN with sparse Mixture-of-Experts layers to scale to trillion parameters efficiently.
peer_reviewed_by: Journal of Machine Learning Research

name: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
description: Introduced FlashAttention, an IO-aware exact attention algorithm that is 2-4x faster and uses less memory than standard attention.
abstract: We present FlashAttention, an IO-aware exact attention algorithm that computes standard attention with significantly fewer memory reads and writes by tiling the computation to exploit GPU SRAM rather than HBM bandwidth, without approximation. FlashAttention is 2–4× faster and 5–20× more memory-efficient than standard attention on modern GPUs by avoiding materializing the full N×N attention matrix in HBM. We demonstrate that IO-awareness—accounting for memory hierarchy in algorithm design—is critical for realizing GPU efficiency, and extend the approach to block-sparse attention for further speed gains. FlashAttention made training on sequences of 16K+ tokens practical and has been universally adopted in Transformer implementations.
key_contribution: IO-aware exact attention via tiling and recomputation; made training long-context Transformers practical.

name: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
description: Abstract We explore optimally training protein language models, an area of significant interest in biological research where guidance on best practices is limited. Most models are trained with extensive compute resources until performance gains plateau, focusing primarily on increasing model sizes rather than optimizing the efficient compute frontier that balances performance and compute budgets. Our investigation is grounded in a massive dataset consisting of 939 million protein sequences.
abstract: Abstract We explore optimally training protein language models, an area of significant interest in biological research where guidance on best practices is limited. Most models are trained with extensive compute resources until performance gains plateau, focusing primarily on increasing model sizes rather than optimizing the efficient compute frontier that balances performance and compute budgets. Our investigation is grounded in a massive dataset consisting of 939 million protein sequences. We trained over 300 models ranging from 3.5 million to 10.7 billion parameters on 5 to 200 billion unique tokens, to investigate the relations between model sizes, training token numbers, and objectives. First, we observed the effect of diminishing returns for the Causal Language Model (CLM) and that of overfitting for the Masked Language Model (MLM) when repeating the commonly used Uniref database. To address this, we included metagenomic protein sequences in the training set to increase the diversity and avoid the plateau or overfitting effects. Second, we obtained the scaling laws of CLM and MLM on Transformer, tailored to the specific characteristics of protein sequence data. Third, we observe a transfer scaling phenomenon from CLM to MLM, further demonstrating the effectiveness of transfer through scaling behaviors based on estimated Effectively Transferred Tokens. Finally, to validate our scaling laws, we compare the large-scale versions of ESM-2 and PROGEN2 on downstream tasks, encompassing evaluations of protein generation as well as structure- and function-related tasks, all within less or equivalent pre-training compute budgets.
key_contribution: Abstract We explore optimally training protein language models, an area of significant interest in biological research where guidance on best practices is limited.

name: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
description: Introduced TruthfulQA, revealing that larger language models are not necessarily more truthful — they reproduce human misconceptions — a critical benchmark for alignment research.
abstract: We propose TruthfulQA, a benchmark to measure whether a language model is truthful in generating answers to questions. The benchmark comprises 817 questions across 38 categories. We find the best model was truthful on 58% of questions versus 94% for humans, and that larger models perform worse.
key_contribution: Benchmark probing whether LLMs generate false but believable answers, measuring truthfulness vs. fluency.

name: GPT-4 Technical Report
description: Technical report describing GPT-4, OpenAI's large multimodal model capable of processing both image and text inputs.
abstract: We report on GPT-4, a large-scale multimodal model that accepts image and text inputs and produces text outputs, achieving human-level performance on a variety of professional and academic benchmarks including the bar exam (top 10%), Biology Olympiad, and AP Chemistry. GPT-4 is post-trained with RLHF and a suite of safety techniques to improve factual accuracy, reduce harmful outputs, and improve instruction-following. We present the Predictable Scaling evaluations, which estimated GPT-4's capabilities from smaller models using scaling laws. GPT-4 represents a qualitative leap in reasoning and knowledge breadth over GPT-3.5 across diverse domains.
key_contribution: Multimodal GPT-4 achieves human-level performance on many professional benchmarks; advanced reasoning and alignment.

name: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
description: Abstract Foundation models pre-trained on web-scale data, such as CLIP, exhibit strong zero-shot visual recognition capabilities. However, their deployment in open-world scenarios is constrained by catastrophic forgetting and an inability to efficiently incorporate novel concepts without full retraining. This paper introduces the Hierarchical Prompt Composition Network (HPC-Net), a memory-efficient architecture that enables vision-language models to learn incrementally in open environments.
abstract: Abstract Foundation models pre-trained on web-scale data, such as CLIP, exhibit strong zero-shot visual recognition capabilities. However, their deployment in open-world scenarios is constrained by catastrophic forgetting and an inability to efficiently incorporate novel concepts without full retraining. This paper introduces the Hierarchical Prompt Composition Network (HPC-Net), a memory-efficient architecture that enables vision-language models to learn incrementally in open environments. HPC-Net maintains a dynamically evolving hierarchy of learnable prompt components that are composed to form task-specific representations while preserving the model's foundational zero-shot capabilities. The architecture exploits the hierarchical compositionality of visual concepts through a three-tier prompt decomposition: (1) foundational prompts encoding broad semantic primitives, (2) compositional prompts for mid-level visual patterns, and (3) instance prompts for category-specific features. A Semantic Prototype Anchoring mechanism is introduced to prevent semantic drift in the shared prompt space, and a Contrastive Prompt Routing module dynamically selects and combines prompts for each input. Extensive experiments across four open-world benchmarks (Split-CIFAR100, Split-ImageNet-R, CORe50, and a new medical imaging benchmark, MedStream-7k) demonstrate that HPC-Net achieves an average accuracy of $84.3 \pm 0.9\%$, a $5.4\%$ absolute improvement over the strongest baseline. This is accomplished while retaining $98.4\%$ of the base model's zero-shot performance on seen domains and requiring only $2.1$M additional parameters (11.6x fewer than adapter-based fusion methods). All code, datasets, and pre-trained models will be released to facilitate reproducibility.
key_contribution: Abstract Foundation models pre-trained on web-scale data, such as CLIP, exhibit strong zero-shot visual recognition capabilities.

name: LLaMA 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models
description: Released LLaMA 2, open-source foundation and instruction-tuned chat models from 7B to 70B parameters.
abstract: We introduce LLaMA 2, a collection of pre-trained and fine-tuned large language models ranging from 7B to 70B parameters, developed as open-weight alternatives to closed commercial models. The chat variants are fine-tuned using supervised fine-tuning followed by iterative RLHF with rejection sampling and proximal policy optimization. LLaMA 2-Chat 70B outperforms most open-source chat models on helpfulness and safety benchmarks and is competitive with ChatGPT. We provide detailed descriptions of our fine-tuning methodology, safety evaluations, and red-teaming procedures, enabling the research community to study and build on the models.
key_contribution: Open-weight LLM family with RLHF-tuned chat variants; enabled open-source research on large chat models.

name: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering
description: Introduced 3D Gaussian Splatting, a real-time neural rendering method using explicit 3D Gaussians as scene representations.
abstract: We present 3D Gaussian Splatting, a novel scene representation that uses a set of anisotropic 3D Gaussians with learned position, covariance, opacity, and spherical harmonic color coefficients as an explicit radiance field, enabling real-time novel view synthesis. The Gaussians are initialized from structure-from-motion point clouds and optimized via differentiable rasterization with adaptive density control that adds or removes Gaussians based on gradient statistics. Our method renders 1080p images at over 100 FPS while achieving quality comparable to or exceeding NeRF-based methods. 3D Gaussian Splatting has rapidly become the dominant approach for real-time photorealistic novel view synthesis.
key_contribution: Real-time novel view synthesis via differentiable 3D Gaussians; orders of magnitude faster than NeRF.
peer_reviewed_by: SIGGRAPH 2023

name: Segment Anything Model (SAM)
description: Introduced SAM, a promptable segmentation foundation model trained on 1B masks, enabling zero-shot segmentation of any object.
abstract: We introduce the Segment Anything Model (SAM), a promptable image segmentation foundation model trained on SA-1B, a dataset of 1 billion masks collected via an interactive data engine. SAM accepts sparse prompts (points, boxes, text) and dense prompts (masks) and produces high-quality segmentation masks for any object in the image in real time. SAM generalizes to new domains zero-shot, including underwater images, CT scans, and egocentric video, with performance competitive with interactive segmentation methods despite seeing no task-specific data. SAM established promptable segmentation as a foundational vision capability analogous to large language model prompting in NLP.
key_contribution: Foundation model for promptable image segmentation trained on 1B masks; zero-shot segmentation.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: Whisper: Robust Speech Recognition via Large-Scale Weak Supervision
description: Introduced Whisper, a robust speech recognition model trained on 680K hours of web audio, enabling strong multilingual ASR.
abstract: We train Whisper, an encoder-decoder Transformer for automatic speech recognition on 680,000 hours of weakly supervised audio-transcript pairs collected from the internet, using the raw transcripts as supervision without significant data cleaning. The model is jointly trained for multilingual speech recognition, speech translation to English, language identification, and voice activity detection as a multitask learning framework. Whisper achieves near-human accuracy on English LibriSpeech and strong performance on accented, noisy, and multilingual speech without dataset-specific fine-tuning. The model's robustness to diverse real-world conditions demonstrates the power of large-scale weak supervision for speech processing.
key_contribution: Weakly supervised ASR on 680k hours of web audio; robust multilingual speech recognition.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: YOLOv8
description: YOLOv8 introduced a new state-of-the-art real-time object detection and segmentation framework by Ultralytics.
abstract: We present YOLOv8, a new family of real-time object detection, segmentation, pose estimation, and classification models that introduces an anchor-free detection head separating objectness and classification into distinct branches, a new C2f backbone module with improved gradient flow, and a decoupled head design for more accurate predictions. YOLOv8 achieves 53.9% mAP on COCO val at 3.2ms A100 TensorRT latency with the large model, representing a favorable accuracy-speed tradeoff. The unified codebase covers detection, instance segmentation, and pose estimation tasks within a consistent API. YOLOv8 has become the most widely adopted YOLO variant for production deployment.
key_contribution: Anchor-free YOLO with improved backbone and head; strong detection, segmentation, and pose in one model.

name: BLIP-2: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training with Frozen LLMs
description: Introduced BLIP-2, efficiently connecting frozen image encoders and LLMs with a lightweight Querying Transformer.
abstract: We present BLIP-2, an efficient vision-language pre-training strategy that bootstraps from frozen pre-trained image encoders and large language models, bridging the modality gap with a lightweight Querying Transformer (Q-Former) trained in two stages: first aligning visual features with text representations, then connecting visual features to a frozen LLM via learned queries.
