BLIP-2 achieves state-of-the-art performance on visual question answering, image captioning, and image-text retrieval with significantly fewer trainable parameters (188M) than prior methods that train the full model. The approach demonstrates that a small trainable adapter can effectively leverage the combined capabilities of a strong frozen vision encoder and LLM. BLIP-2 established the Q-Former as an efficient paradigm for vision-language model construction.
key_contribution: Frozen LLM connected to frozen image encoder via lightweight Q-Former; efficient vision-language pre-training.
peer_reviewed_by: International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

name: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces
description: Introduced Mamba, a selective state space model achieving linear-time sequence modeling with performance matching Transformers.
abstract: We present Mamba, a selective state space model (SSM) that addresses the efficiency limitations of Transformers for long sequences by making SSM parameters functions of the input, enabling the model to selectively propagate or forget information based on the current token. The selective mechanism is implemented via a hardware-aware parallel scan algorithm in CUDA that achieves inference speeds 5× faster than Transformers of equivalent size. Mamba matches or outperforms Transformers of the same size on language modeling, DNA, and audio tasks while scaling linearly in sequence length. Mamba demonstrates that SSMs with selectivity can be a powerful alternative to attention for sequence modeling.
key_contribution: Selective state space model with input-dependent transitions achieves Transformer quality at linear time complexity.

name: ControlNet: Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion
description: Introduced ControlNet for adding spatial conditioning to diffusion models, enabling precise control over generated images.
abstract: We present ControlNet, a neural network architecture that adds spatial conditioning controls—such as edge maps, depth maps, segmentation maps, and human pose—to large pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models by creating a trainable copy of the encoding layers of the diffusion model connected to the frozen backbone via zero-initialized convolutions. Training on task-specific datasets with as few as 50,000 samples enables precise control over the structure and composition of generated images while preserving the model's generative quality. ControlNet enables structural conditioning previously impossible with text prompts alone, achieving strong results on pose-guided, edge-guided, and depth-guided generation. ControlNet became the standard approach for spatially conditioned image generation.
key_contribution: Trainable copy of UNet encoder conditions diffusion models on spatial inputs (depth, pose, edge) precisely.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)

name: DreamBooth: Fine Tuning Text-to-Image Models for Subject-Driven Generation
description: Introduced DreamBooth for personalizing text-to-image diffusion models with just 3-5 images of a subject.
abstract: We present DreamBooth, a method for personalizing text-to-image diffusion models to a specific subject using as few as 3–5 photographs. A rare token identifier is bound to the subject by fine-tuning the full diffusion model with a class-specific prior preservation loss that prevents language drift and maintains the model's prior over the subject class. The resulting model generates the specific subject in diverse contexts, poses, and styles specified by text prompts while preserving its identity. DreamBooth enables personalized generation, recontextualization, and accessorization of real subjects, and has become the foundation for a large ecosystem of subject-driven image generation applications.
key_contribution: Fine-tunes text-to-image models on 3-5 images to bind subject identity to a rare token; personalized generation.
peer_reviewed_by: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)

name: LLaVA: Visual Instruction Tuning
description: Introduced LLaVA, connecting CLIP and LLaMA for visual instruction tuning, enabling strong multimodal conversations.
abstract: We present LLaVA (Large Language and Vision Assistant), a visual instruction tuning approach that generates multimodal instruction-following data using GPT-4 and fine-tunes a combination of a visual encoder (CLIP ViT-L/14) connected to a language model (Vicuna) via a linear projection layer. Using 158K generated multi-turn conversation, detailed description, and complex reasoning examples, LLaVA demonstrates impressive multimodal chat capabilities and zero-shot task transfer to unseen tasks. On Science QA, LLaVA+GPT-4 achieves 92.53% accuracy, exceeding GPT-4 alone. LLaVA established efficient instruction tuning with synthesized data as the key approach for building open-source multimodal language models.
key_contribution: Visual instruction tuning with GPT-4-generated data connects CLIP to LLaMA for instruction-following VQA.

name: Direct Preference Optimization
description: Introduced DPO, a simpler alternative to RLHF that directly fine-tunes LLMs from human preference data without a reward model.
abstract: We present Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a simple and stable alternative to RLHF for aligning language models to human preferences that directly optimizes the policy to satisfy preference data without training an explicit reward model or using reinforcement learning. We show that the RLHF objective can be re-parameterized such that the optimal policy for a given reward function has a closed form, allowing us to derive a classification loss on preference pairs that directly trains the policy. DPO achieves competitive performance with PPO-based RLHF on sentiment control, summarization, and dialogue tasks while being simpler to implement, more computationally efficient, and more stable. DPO became the dominant alternative to RLHF for preference-based fine-tuning.
key_contribution: Closed-form solution that optimizes preference data without RL; simpler and stabler alternative to RLHF.

name: DINOv2: Learning Robust Visual Features without Supervision
description: Introduced DINOv2, self-supervised ViT features that are universal visual representations applicable across diverse vision tasks.
abstract: We present DINOv2, a method for learning robust visual features by training Vision Transformers with a combination of image-level and patch-level self-supervised objectives on a curated dataset of 142 million high-quality images (LVD-142M) assembled through automatic curation from web-crawled data. The features trained with DINOv2 work out of the box as visual features for tasks requiring both image-level understanding (classification, retrieval) and pixel-level understanding (segmentation, depth) with a simple linear layer or nearest-neighbor classifier, without any fine-tuning. DINOv2 achieves strong performance on all benchmarks, surpassing weakly supervised models like CLIP on most tasks. It established large-scale curated self-supervised pre-training as a path to universal visual features.
key_contribution: Self-supervised ViT trained on curated data produces universal dense visual features for many downstream tasks.
peer_reviewed_by: Transactions on Machine Learning Research

name: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
description: Human feedback can prevent overtly harmful utterances in conversational models, but may not automatically mitigate subtle problematic behaviors such as a stated desire for self-preservation or power. Constitutional AI offers an alternative, replacing human feedback with feedback from AI models conditioned only on a list of written principles. We find this approach effectively prevents the expression of such behaviors.
abstract: Human feedback can prevent overtly harmful utterances in conversational models, but may not automatically mitigate subtle problematic behaviors such as a stated desire for self-preservation or power. Constitutional AI offers an alternative, replacing human feedback with feedback from AI models conditioned only on a list of written principles. We find this approach effectively prevents the expression of such behaviors. The success of simple principles motivates us to ask: can models learn general ethical behaviors from only a single written principle? To test this, we run experiments using a principle roughly stated as "do what's best for humanity". We find that the largest dialogue models can generalize from this short constitution, resulting in harmless assistants with no stated interest in specific motivations like power. A general principle may thus partially avoid the need for a long list of constitutions targeting potentially harmful behaviors. However, more detailed constitutions still improve fine-grained control over specific types of harms. This suggests both general and specific principles have value for steering AI safely.
key_contribution: Human feedback can prevent overtly harmful utterances in conversational models, but may not automatically mitigate subtle problematic behaviors such as a stated desire for self-preservation or power.

name: GraphCast: Learning skillful medium-range global weather forecasting
description: Global medium-range weather forecasting is critical to decision-making across many social and economic domains. Traditional numerical weather prediction uses increased compute resources to improve forecast accuracy, but cannot directly use historical weather data to improve the underlying model. We introduce a machine learning-based method called "GraphCast", which can be trained directly from reanalysis data. It predicts hundreds of weather variables, over 10 days at 0.25 degree resolution globally, in under one minute. We show that GraphCast significantly outperforms the most accurate operational deterministic systems on 90% of 1380 verification targets, and its forecasts support better severe event prediction, including tropical cyclones, atmospheric rivers, and extreme temperatures. GraphCast is a key advance in accurate and efficient weather forecasting, and helps realize the promise of machine learning for modeling complex dynamical systems.
abstract: Global medium-range weather forecasting is critical to decision-making across many social and economic domains. Traditional numerical weather prediction uses increased compute resources to improve forecast accuracy, but cannot directly use historical weather data to improve the underlying model. We introduce a machine learning-based method called "GraphCast", which can be trained directly from reanalysis data. It predicts hundreds of weather variables, over 10 days at 0.25 degree resolution globally, in under one minute. We show that GraphCast significantly outperforms the most accurate operational deterministic systems on 90% of 1380 verification targets, and its forecasts support better severe event prediction, including tropical cyclones, atmospheric rivers, and extreme temperatures. GraphCast is a key advance in accurate and efficient weather forecasting, and helps realize the promise of machine learning for modeling complex dynamical systems.
key_contribution: Introduces GraphCast, a learned global weather forecasting model achieving strong medium-range performance.

name: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3
description: Extended AlphaFold to predict structures of all biomolecular interactions including proteins, nucleic acids, ligands, and their complexes.
abstract: The introduction of AlphaFold 2 has spurred a revolution in modelling the structure of proteins and their interactions, enabling a huge range of applications in protein biology and drug discovery. Here we describe our multimodal approach for predicting the joint structure of complexes including proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, ions and modified residues.
key_contribution: Introduces AlphaFold 3 for predicting biomolecular complexes, extending structure prediction beyond single proteins.
peer_reviewed_by: Nature

name: Mixtral of Experts
description: Introduced Mixtral 8x7B, a sparse mixture-of-experts model that outperforms Llama 2 70B at a fraction of the inference cost.
abstract: We introduce Mixtral 8×7B, a sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) language model in which each Transformer layer contains 8 expert feed-forward networks and a router that selects the top 2 experts for each token, resulting in active parameters of ~13B per token despite a total of 46.7B parameters. Mixtral matches or outperforms LLaMA 2 70B and GPT-3.5 on most standard benchmarks while using ~6× fewer FLOPs per token, and a fine-tuned Mixtral 8×7B Instruct sets a new state of the art among open models on human preference evaluations. We release the model weights openly, enabling the research community to study and build on sparse MoE architectures. Mixtral demonstrates that sparse routing with multiple experts is a practical and highly efficient architecture for large language models.
key_contribution: Sparse MoE with 8 experts and top-2 routing matches 70B dense models at the compute cost of 13B.

name: Gemini: A Family of Highly Capable Multimodal Models
description: Introduces Gemini, Google DeepMind's family of natively multimodal models (Ultra, Pro, Nano) trained on text, image, audio, and video. Gemini Ultra surpasses GPT-4 on MMLU and sets new state-of-the-art across 30 of 32 benchmarks, representing the first model to achieve human-expert performance on massive multitask language understanding.
abstract: This report introduces a new family of multimodal models, Gemini, that exhibit remarkable capabilities across image, audio, video, and text understanding. The Gemini family consists of Ultra, Pro, and Nano sizes, suitable for a wide range of tasks. Gemini Ultra achieves state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of benchmarks including text and coding.
key_contribution: First natively multimodal foundation model family surpassing GPT-4 on MMLU
